1973.fun_fact: The lead single from George Harrison's Living in the Material World album spent five weeks at #1 and was driven by Harrison's distinctive slide guitar work and his commitment to the Hare Krishna philosophy.

1973.title: If You Want Me to Stay
1973.fun_fact: One of the final major hits from Sly Stone's commercial peak, this reached #12 on the Hot 100 and showcased his ability to create deeply funky grooves even as his band's personnel became increasingly unstable.

1973.title: Daddy's Home
1973.fun_fact: Jermaine Jackson's cover of Shep and the Limelites' 1961 doo-wop hit reached #9 on the Hot 100 and was his biggest solo hit during his years on Motown before his brothers moved to Epic Records.

1973.title: Neither One of Us (Wants to Be the First to Say Goodbye)
1973.fun_fact: Jim Weatherly's beautifully constructed farewell song spent two weeks at #1 on the R&B chart and reached #2 on the Hot 100, winning the Grammy for Best Pop Vocal Performance by a Duo or Group.

1973.title: I'm Doin' Fine Now
1973.fun_fact: This Philadelphia soul production by Thom Bell reached #17 on the Hot 100 and was the biggest hit for New York City, a group whose smooth harmonies anticipated the quiet storm R&B sound of the late 1970s.

1973.title: Could It Be I'm Falling in Love
1973.fun_fact: Produced by Thom Bell at Philadelphia International, this reached #4 on the Hot 100 and was one of the Spinners' most beloved recordings from their remarkably productive early 1970s collaboration with Bell.

1973.title: Daniel
1973.fun_fact: Written by Bernie Taupin from a newspaper article about Vietnam veterans returning home, this reached #2 on the Hot 100 and Elton John has described it as one of his personal favorite recordings.

1973.title: Midnight Train to Georgia
1973.fun_fact: Originally written by Jim Weatherly as 'Midnight Plane to Houston' about a trip to see football player Joe Namath, this spent two weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 and won the Grammy for Best R&B Performance.

1973.title: Smoke on the Water
1973.fun_fact: Written about the burning of a casino in Montreux, Switzerland when Frank Zappa's concert audience set it on fire, this reached #4 on the Hot 100 and features one of rock's most recognizable guitar riffs.

1973.title: The Cover of 'Rolling Stone'
1973.fun_fact: Written by Shel Silverstein as a satirical commentary on rock star ambitions, this irony-rich track reached #6 on the Hot 100 and eventually resulted in Dr. Hook actually appearing on the cover of Rolling Stone magazine.

1973.title: Behind Closed Doors
1973.fun_fact: This Kenny O'Dell composition became Charlie Rich's signature recording, spending two weeks at #1 on the country chart and reaching #15 on the Hot 100 in a major pop crossover moment.

1973.title: Your Mama Don't Dance
1973.fun_fact: Written and recorded in one session, this nostalgic rock and roll tribute reached #4 on the Hot 100 and was one of Loggins and Messina's most energetic departures from their usual country-folk style.

1973.title: Feelin' Stronger Every Day
1973.fun_fact: Written by Peter Cetera and James Pankow about the aftermath of a difficult relationship, this reached #10 on the Hot 100 and was part of Chicago's extraordinarily productive early period.

1973.title: The Cisco Kid
1973.fun_fact: War named this funky track after the classic Western television character and reached #2 on the Hot 100 with a sound that effortlessly blended rock, funk, and Latin influences.

1973.title: Live and Let Die
1973.fun_fact: Paul McCartney's James Bond theme reached #2 on the Hot 100 and became the first Bond theme to be nominated for an Academy Award for Best Original Song, despite ultimately losing to 'The Way We Were.'

1973.title: Oh, Babe, What Would You Say?
1973.fun_fact: EMI recording engineer Norman Smith, who had worked with The Beatles during the Sgt. Pepper era, recorded this vaudeville-flavored pop track under the name Hurricane Smith and reached #3 on the Hot 100.

1973.title: I Believe in You (You Believe in Me)
1973.fun_fact: Produced by Don Davis in Detroit, this reached #11 on the Hot 100 and was one of several hits Johnnie Taylor had during his productive early 1970s period at Stax Records in Memphis.

1973.title: Sing
1973.fun_fact: Originally written by Joe Raposo for the children's television program Sesame Street, The Carpenters' version reached #3 on the Hot 100 and featured a children's chorus that gave it a distinctly wholesome charm.

1973.title: Ain't No Woman (Like the One I've Got)
1973.fun_fact: Produced by Thom Bell for Dunhill Records after the Four Tops left Motown, this reached #4 on the Hot 100 and proved the group could achieve major commercial success outside their longtime home label.

1973.title: Dueling Banjos
1973.fun_fact: From the film Deliverance, this traditional bluegrass piece reached #2 on the Hot 100 in early 1973 and sparked a nationwide revival of interest in bluegrass and old-time American acoustic music.

1973.title: Higher Ground
1973.fun_fact: Stevie Wonder wrote, arranged, and recorded this entire track himself in one afternoon, creating one of the most innovative funk recordings of the decade; the clavinet riff later inspired countless producers.

1973.title: Here I Am (Come and Take Me)
1973.fun_fact: Al Green's continued commercial dominance into 1973 is evidenced by this charting hit, which reached #10 on the Hot 100 and demonstrated that his partnership with Willie Mitchell remained extraordinarily productive.

1973.title: My Maria
1973.fun_fact: Written by Daniel Moore and B.W. Stevenson, this breezy pop track reached #9 on the Hot 100 for its obscure Texas singer; B.J. Thomas and Brooks & Dunn would later take cover versions to new heights of success.

1973.title: Superfly
1973.fun_fact: The title track from Curtis Mayfield's groundbreaking blaxploitation soundtrack reached #8 on the Hot 100 and the album from which it came is widely regarded as one of the greatest soundtrack recordings in history.

1973.title: Last Song
1973.fun_fact: This Canadian pop group's biggest hit reached #3 on the Hot 100 and was one of several Canadian artists who scored unexpected American crossover success during the early 1970s.

1973.title: Get Down
1973.fun_fact: Gilbert O'Sullivan's second major US hit reached #7 on the Hot 100 and was written in response to a dog that kept jumping on his bed; its cheerful, piano-led arrangement contrasted sharply with his darker previous material.

1973.title: Reelin' in the Years
1973.fun_fact: Featuring session guitarist Elliot Randall on a guitar solo that both Walter Becker and Donald Fagen have praised as the greatest guitar solo ever to appear on a Steely Dan recording, this reached #11 on the Hot 100.

1973.title: Hocus Pocus
1973.fun_fact: Dutch progressive rock band Focus created this extraordinary track featuring virtuoso guitarist Jan Akkerman and vocalist Thijs van Leer's operatic yodeling, reaching #9 on the Hot 100.

1973.title: Yesterday Once More
1973.fun_fact: Richard Carpenter's nostalgic meditation on the golden age of radio reached #2 on the Hot 100 and was the Carpenters' most personal recording, a love letter to the AM pop music that had shaped their musical upbringing.

1973.title: Boogie Woogie Bugle Boy
1973.fun_fact: Bette Midler's theatrical revival of the Andrews Sisters' 1941 Andrews Sisters hit was recorded live at the Continental Baths in New York and reached #8 on the Hot 100, launching her recording career.

1973.title: Clair
1973.fun_fact: Written about Gilbert O'Sullivan's manager Gordon Mills's young daughter Claire, this became his biggest global hit, spending two weeks at #2 on the Hot 100 and several weeks at #1 in the UK.

1973.title: Do It Again
1973.fun_fact: Built on a hypnotic Latin groove and featuring one of rock's most distinctive opening synthesizer basslines, this debut Steely Dan single reached #6 on the Hot 100 and introduced their sophisticated jazz-rock sound.

1973.title: Kodachrome
1973.fun_fact: Paul Simon's nostalgic celebration of colorful memory was briefly banned by BBC Radio for its use of a brand name, but reached #2 on the Hot 100 and won him a Grammy nomination.

1973.title: Why Can't We Live Together
1973.fun_fact: Recorded using only a Lowrey organ and drum machine with no other instruments, this minimalist anti-war protest song reached #3 on the Hot 100 and remains a remarkable example of achieving maximum emotional impact with minimal means.

1973.title: Do You Want to Dance?
1973.fun_fact: Bette Midler's exuberant cover of Bobby Freeman's 1958 rock and roll classic reached #17 on the Hot 100 and was one of several vintage-flavored recordings that defined her early image as 'The Divine Miss M.'

1973.title: So Very Hard to Go
1973.fun_fact: This Oakland funk and soul ensemble's biggest pop hit reached #17 on the Hot 100 and featured one of the tightest horn sections in popular music, influencing countless later funk and R&B recordings.

1973.title: Rockin' Pneumonia and the Boogie Woogie Flu
1973.fun_fact: Johnny Rivers' cover of Huey 'Piano' Smith's 1957 New Orleans R&B classic reached #6 on the Hot 100 and was part of his late-career comeback strategy of revisiting earlier rock and roll material.

1973.title: Ramblin' Man
1973.fun_fact: The Allman Brothers Band's biggest commercial hit reached #2 on the Hot 100 and was written by guitarist Dickey Betts, not the group's late founding leader Duane Allman as many listeners assumed.

1973.title: Masterpiece
1973.fun_fact: Produced by Norman Whitfield, this extended soul epic reached #7 on the Hot 100 and was part of Whitfield's ambitious concept of long-form psychedelic soul that he developed with the Temptations.

1973.title: Peaceful
1973.fun_fact: Written by Kenny Rankin, this gentle pop ballad gave Helen Reddy another top 20 hit in 1973 as she built one of the most consistent run of hits of any female solo artist during this period.

1973.title: One of a Kind (Love Affair)
1973.fun_fact: Another Thom Bell-produced gem, this reached #11 on the Hot 100 and demonstrated Bell and the Spinners' ability to craft exquisitely arranged soul ballads with an almost orchestral complexity.

1973.title: Funny Face
1973.fun_fact: The follow-up to 'The Happiest Girl in the Whole U.S.A.,' Donna Fargo's second major country-pop crossover reached #5 on the Hot 100 and solidified her as one of the year's most bankable new acts.

1973.title: Funky Worm
1973.fun_fact: Known for its cartoonish synthesizer riff performed by Walter 'Junie' Morrison, this reached #15 on the Hot 100 and was later sampled by Dr. Dre in 'Nuthin' But a G Thang,' giving the Ohio Players an unlikely hip-hop legacy.

1973.title: Angie
1973.fun_fact: Keith Richards wrote this acoustic ballad during heroin rehabilitation in Switzerland; it spent two weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 and stands as one of the Stones' most unexpectedly tender recordings.

1973.title: Jambalaya (On the Bayou)
1973.fun_fact: John Fogerty recorded this Hank Williams cover under the pseudonym Blue Ridge Rangers as a tribute to country music during a period when he was creatively withdrawing from Creedence Clearwater Revival.

1973.title: Don't Expect Me to Be Your Friend
1973.fun_fact: This follow-up to 'Me and You and a Dog Named Boo' reached #8 on the Hot 100 and continued Lobo's run of laid-back, conversational pop hits that made him one of the early 1970s' most reliable soft rock artists.

1973.title: Break Up to Make Up
1973.fun_fact: Produced and written by Thom Bell and Linda Creed, this sophisticated Philly soul ballad reached #5 on the Hot 100 and stands as one of the era's finest examples of Bell's gift for emotionally nuanced production.

1973.title: Daisy a Day
1973.fun_fact: This sentimental folk song about a lifelong romance reached #14 on the Hot 100 and was one of the more unlikely hits of 1973, performed by a Maine comedian and folk singer better known for his work on Laugh-In.

1973.title: Also Sprach Zarathustra (2001)
1973.fun_fact: Brazilian arranger Eumir Deodato's jazz-funk reimagining of the Richard Strauss fanfare made famous by 2001: A Space Odyssey reached #2 on the Hot 100 and won the Grammy for Best Pop Instrumental Performance.

1973.title: Stir It Up
1973.fun_fact: Johnny Nash's recording of Bob Marley's song introduced the future reggae legend's composition to American audiences, reaching #12 on the Hot 100 and predating Marley's own international breakthrough by several years.

1973.title: Money
1973.fun_fact: The most commercially successful track from The Dark Side of the Moon album, this reached #13 on the Hot 100 in its edited single form and introduced the album's themes of capitalism and greed to mainstream radio audiences.

1973.title: Gypsy Man
1973.fun_fact: One of two War songs on the 1973 year-end chart, this atmospheric funk track reached #8 on the Hot 100 and demonstrated the band's remarkable diversity across Latin, jazz, R&B, and rock musical territories.

1973.title: The World Is a Ghetto
1973.fun_fact: The title track from War's best-selling album reached #7 on the Hot 100 and the album itself spent two weeks at #1 on the Billboard 200, making it one of the best-selling albums of 1973.

1973.title: Yes We Can Can
1973.fun_fact: The Pointer Sisters' debut single reached #11 on the Hot 100 and introduced their retro-soul style to a national audience; the song was written by Allen Toussaint and its groove clearly bears his distinctive New Orleans fingerprint.

1973.title: Free Ride
1973.fun_fact: Written by Dan Hartman, who would later become a solo star himself, this hard rock track reached #14 on the Hot 100 and featured the kind of arena-ready riff that defined rock radio in the early 1970s.

1973.title: Space Oddity
1973.fun_fact: Originally released in 1969 to coincide with the Apollo 11 moon landing, David Bowie's tale of Major Tom reached #15 on the Hot 100 five years later during a period of intense American interest in his glam rock persona.

1973.title: It Never Rains in Southern California
1973.fun_fact: British songwriter Albert Hammond wrote this about his own disappointments trying to break into the music industry in Los Angeles, and it reached #5 on the Hot 100 in one of 1972's more ironic success stories.

1973.title: The Twelfth of Never
1973.fun_fact: Donny Osmond's cover of the 1957 Johnny Mathis classic reached #8 on the Hot 100 and demonstrated his talent for interpreting classic ballad material with a sincerity unusual for a teenage pop idol.

1973.title: Papa Was a Rollin' Stone
1973.fun_fact: This nearly twelve-minute psychedelic soul epic was edited to under four minutes for single release, but still won three Grammy Awards including Record of the Year, making it one of the most acclaimed Temptations recordings.

1974.title: The Way We Were
1974.fun_fact: Marvin Hamlisch composed this theme for the Robert Redford and Barbra Streisand film, and the recording spent three weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 while winning Academy Awards for both Best Original Song and Best Original Score.

1974.title: Seasons in the Sun
1974.fun_fact: Adapted from Jacques Brel's 'Le Moribond' with English lyrics by Rod McKuen, this melancholy farewell song spent three weeks at #1 and sold over six million copies worldwide despite Terry Jacks being a virtually unknown Canadian musician.

1974.title: Love's Theme
1974.fun_fact: Barry White's 40-piece orchestral ensemble created this lush instrumental that spent one week at #1, making it one of the few instrumental recordings to top the Hot 100 during the 1970s.

1974.title: Come and Get Your Love
1974.fun_fact: Written by Pat Vegas of Redbone, this irresistible funk-rock track reached #5 on the Hot 100 and gained an entirely new generation of fans when Marvel used it as the opening song of Guardians of the Galaxy in 2014.

1974.title: Dancing Machine
1974.fun_fact: The Jackson 5's first major hit in two years reached #2 on the Hot 100 and featured a young Michael Jackson popularizing the robot dance move on American Bandstand, launching a nationwide dance craze.

1974.title: The Loco-Motion
1974.fun_fact: Grand Funk Railroad's hard rock cover of Little Eva's 1962 Carole King-written hit spent two weeks at #1 and became the second version of the song to reach #1 on the Hot 100, a remarkable achievement for any composition.

1974.title: TSOP (The Sound of Philadelphia)
1974.fun_fact: The official theme song for Soul Train, this Kenny Gamble and Leon Huff production spent two weeks at #1 and became the sonic calling card for the entire Philadelphia International Records sound.

1974.title: The Streak
1974.fun_fact: Ray Stevens's comic novelty song about the streaking craze that briefly swept American college campuses spent three weeks at #1 and was his second #1 hit following 'Everything Is Beautiful' in 1970.

1974.title: Bennie and the Jets
1974.fun_fact: Originally not intended as a single, Elton John's theatrical tribute to rock stardom was added to US radio playlists by popular demand and spent two weeks at #1, making it a landmark of early 1970s arena rock mythology.

1974.title: One Hell of a Woman
1974.fun_fact: Mac Davis's follow-up to 'Baby Don't Get Hooked on Me' reached #11 on the Hot 100 and maintained his profile as both a pop recording artist and a popular television personality with his own variety show.

1974.title: Until You Come Back to Me (That's What I'm Gonna Do)
1974.fun_fact: Written by Stevie Wonder, Clarence Paul, and Morris Broadnax in 1967 but not released until Aretha Franklin recorded it, this reached #3 on the Hot 100 and became one of her most beloved later Motown-affiliated recordings.

1974.title: Jungle Boogie
1974.fun_fact: This primal funk track with its distinctive chant reached #4 on the Hot 100 and later became one of the most recognizable opening themes in cinema when Quentin Tarantino used it in Pulp Fiction.

1974.title: Midnight at the Oasis
1974.fun_fact: David Nichtern wrote this gentle desert fantasy about a romantic encounter, and Maria Muldaur's warm, intimate vocal took it to #6 on the Hot 100 in what became her signature recording.

1974.title: You Make Me Feel Brand New
1974.fun_fact: Written by Thom Bell and Linda Creed, this luxurious Philly soul ballad reached #2 on the Hot 100 and featured Russell Thompkins Jr.'s extraordinary falsetto in what many consider the Stylistics' finest recording.

1974.title: Show and Tell
1974.fun_fact: Produced by Jerry Fuller, Al Wilson's smooth pop-soul recording spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100 and was the biggest hit for this California singer, who had previously charted with 'The Snake' in 1968.

1974.title: Spiders and Snakes
1974.fun_fact: This comedic country novelty song about childhood romance reached #3 on the Hot 100 and was the breakthrough hit for Jim Stafford, a Florida comedian who briefly hosted his own network television variety show.

1974.title: Rock On
1974.fun_fact: British actor and singer David Essex created this atmospheric glam rock track using an unconventional production approach with double bass, cellos, and minimal drums; it reached #5 on the Hot 100.

1974.title: Sunshine on My Shoulders
1974.fun_fact: Written by John Denver in Minneapolis during a grey winter day as a meditation on the life-giving power of sunlight, this reached #1 on the Hot 100 and won the Grammy for Best Male Pop Vocal Performance.

1974.title: Sideshow
1974.fun_fact: This Philadelphia vocal group's debut single reached #8 on the Hot 100 and became one of the most distinctive soul recordings of 1974, featuring Bobby Eli's distinctive production and Norman Harris's guitar work.

1974.title: Hooked on a Feeling
1974.fun_fact: Swedish band Blue Swede's cover of B.J. Thomas's 1969 hit added the now-iconic 'ooga chaka' chant intro borrowed from Jonathan King's earlier version, spending one week at #1 and later becoming famous through the Guardians of the Galaxy trailer.

1974.title: Billy Don't Be a Hero
1974.fun_fact: Originally a UK hit for Paper Lace, Bo Donaldson's American cover of this anti-war narrative reached #1 on the Hot 100 and outsold the original in the United States by a significant margin.

1974.title: Band on the Run
1974.fun_fact: Recorded in Lagos, Nigeria despite McCartney being warned it was dangerous to record there, this ambitious suite spent three weeks at #1 and helped reestablish McCartney's critical reputation after some disappointing post-Beatles output.

1974.title: The Most Beautiful Girl
1974.fun_fact: Following the massive success of 'Behind Closed Doors,' this gentle ballad spent two weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 and made Charlie Rich one of the few country artists to achieve simultaneous pop and country chart domination.

1974.title: Time in a Bottle
1974.fun_fact: Released posthumously after Jim Croce's death in September 1973, this beautiful meditation on wishing to save happy moments forever spent two weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 and became one of his most beloved recordings.

1974.title: Annie's Song
1974.fun_fact: John Denver wrote this tribute to his wife Annie Adams in about ten minutes while riding a ski lift in Aspen; it spent two weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 and remains one of pop music's most beloved romantic dedications.

1974.title: Let Me Be There
1974.fun_fact: Olivia Newton-John's US breakthrough single reached #6 on the Hot 100, won her the Grammy for Best Female Country Vocal Performance, and launched one of the most commercially successful careers in 1970s pop music.

1974.title: Sundown
1974.fun_fact: Written about his jealousy over a former girlfriend, this spent two weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 and was Gordon Lightfoot's only chart-topper, produced with an unusually dark and menacing undertone for his typically gentle folk style.

1974.title: (You're) Having My Baby
1974.fun_fact: Paul Anka wrote this controversial song about his wife's pregnancy and the feminist organization NOW publicly condemned it as sexist, which only increased sales; the song spent three weeks at #1 on the Hot 100.

1974.title: Rock Me Gently
1974.fun_fact: Canadian singer Andy Kim had co-written 'Sugar Sugar' for The Archies in 1969; this self-written glam pop track reached #1 on the Hot 100 and was the biggest solo hit of his career.

1974.title: Boogie Down
1974.fun_fact: Former Temptations lead singer Eddie Kendricks followed his 1973 hit 'Keep on Truckin' with this funkier sequel that reached #2 on the Hot 100 and #1 on the R&B chart.

1974.title: You're Sixteen
1974.fun_fact: Ringo Starr's cover of Johnny Burnette's 1960 hit spent two weeks at #1 and featured Paul McCartney playing a kazoo solo, marking one of the more unusual inter-Beatle musical collaborations of the solo era.

1974.title: If You Love Me (Let Me Know)
1974.fun_fact: The second of two Olivia Newton-John songs in the 1974 year-end Hot 100, this reached #5 on the Hot 100 and continued her remarkable run of pop-country crossover success.

1974.title: Dark Lady
1974.fun_fact: Cher's third consecutive #1 single made her the first female solo artist to have three consecutive #1 hits on the Hot 100, demonstrating her remarkable chart dominance during this period.

1974.title: Best Thing That Ever Happened to Me
1974.fun_fact: Written by Jim Weatherly, this gorgeous Buddah Records soul ballad reached #3 on the Hot 100 and was part of Gladys Knight's extraordinary run of charting hits following her move from Motown.

1974.title: Feel Like Makin' Love
1974.fun_fact: Co-written by Eugene McDaniels, Roberta Flack's sensual recording spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100 and won the Grammy for Best R&B Song, demonstrating her ability to work across multiple soul and pop styles.

1974.title: Just Don't Want to Be Lonely
1974.fun_fact: This lush Thom Bell-influenced production reached #10 on the Hot 100 and was one of the Main Ingredient's final major hits as the group's commercial momentum began to slow after Cuba Gooding Sr.'s departure.

1974.title: Nothing from Nothing
1974.fun_fact: Billy Preston's philosophical funk track spent one week at #1 and contained the memorable lyric 'nothing from nothing leaves nothing,' which became one of the era's most quoted pop aphorisms.

1974.title: Rock Your Baby
1974.fun_fact: Produced by Harry Wayne Casey and Richard Finch of KC and the Sunshine Band as a demo for their own group, they gave it to George McCrae instead; it spent two weeks at #1 and is considered one of disco's foundational recordings.

1974.title: Top of the World
1974.fun_fact: Written by Richard Carpenter and John Bettis, this sunny country-pop song spent two weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 and was a deliberate attempt to break into the country radio market, which it accomplished with great success.

1974.title: The Joker
1974.fun_fact: Steve Miller wrote this playful self-portrait as a 'space cowboy, gangster of love, and midnight toker' and it spent one week at #1; the guitar lick had originally appeared in his earlier song 'The Gangster of Love.'

1974.title: I've Got to Use My Imagination
1974.fun_fact: Written by Gerry Goffin and Barry Goldberg, this Buddah Records recording reached #4 on the Hot 100 and featured a propulsive, horn-driven arrangement that made it one of Gladys Knight's most energetic recordings.

1974.title: The Show Must Go On
1974.fun_fact: Written by Leo Sayer and David Courtney as a theatrical anthem about perseverance through hardship, Three Dog Night's version reached #4 on the Hot 100 in what became one of their final major chart entries.

1974.title: Rock the Boat
1974.fun_fact: Generally considered one of the first true disco hits, this spent one week at #1 in the summer of 1974 and its success convinced record labels that the emerging underground dance music scene had mainstream commercial potential.

1974.title: Smokin' in the Boys Room
1974.fun_fact: This hard rock celebration of teenage rebellion reached #3 on the Hot 100 and later became a #1 hit for Mötley Crüe, giving Brownsville Station an unexpected legacy as pioneer heavy metal songwriters.

1974.title: Living for the City
1974.fun_fact: Stevie Wonder's seven-minute political epic about urban poverty and systemic racism was edited to under four minutes for radio; even so, its raw power reached #8 on the Hot 100 and won the Grammy for Best R&B Song.

1974.title: Then Came You
1974.fun_fact: Produced by Thom Bell, this pairing of Dionne Warwick with the Spinners was an inspired match of vocal talents that spent one week at #1 and became Warwick's only chart-topper as a solo or collaborative artist.

1974.title: The Night Chicago Died
1974.fun_fact: British pop group Paper Lace's fictional account of a battle between Al Capone's gang and the Chicago police spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100, though Chicago historians noted numerous historical inaccuracies in the lyrics.

1974.title: The Entertainer
1974.fun_fact: Adapted from Scott Joplin's 1902 ragtime composition for the film The Sting, Hamlisch's modern arrangement reached #3 on the Hot 100 and sparked a nationwide revival of interest in Scott Joplin's ragtime catalog.

1974.title: Waterloo
1974.fun_fact: ABBA's Eurovision Song Contest winner reached #6 on the Hot 100 and marked the Swedish quartet's first significant US chart entry, though their massive American breakthrough would not come until several years later.

1974.title: The Air That I Breathe
1974.fun_fact: Written by Albert Hammond and Mike Hazlewood, this breathtaking ballad reached #6 on the Hot 100 and was later at the center of a plagiarism dispute when Radiohead's 'Creep' was found to share substantial melodic similarities.

1974.title: Rikki Don't Lose That Number
1974.fun_fact: The opening piano riff is borrowed from Horace Silver's jazz composition 'Song for My Father,' this reached #4 on the Hot 100 and was the first Steely Dan single to crack the top 5.

1974.title: Mockingbird
1974.fun_fact: This playful duet from two of music's most famous romantic partners reached #5 on the Hot 100 and was recorded during a period when Simon and Taylor's real-life marriage was one of popular culture's most scrutinized relationships.

1974.title: Help Me
1974.fun_fact: Joni Mitchell's most commercially successful single reached #7 on the Hot 100 and was her only top 10 hit, driven by Tom Scott's prominent saxophone work that gave it a warm jazz-pop feel unusual in her catalog.

1974.title: You Won't See Me
1974.fun_fact: Anne Murray's cover of The Beatles' 1965 Help! album track reached #8 on the Hot 100 and demonstrated her skill at transforming rock and roll material into polished country-pop crossover hits.

1974.title: Never, Never Gonna Give You Up
1974.fun_fact: Barry White's deep-voiced romantic declaration reached #7 on the Hot 100 and was part of his extraordinary commercial run during which he placed multiple hits per year on the charts through sheer prolific output.

1974.title: Tell Me Something Good
1974.fun_fact: Written by Stevie Wonder specifically for Rufus and delivered with explosive power by Chaka Khan, this reached #3 on the Hot 100 and won the Grammy for Best R&B Song, launching Khan's reputation as one of the era's great voices.

1974.title: You and Me Against the World
1974.fun_fact: Written by Paul Williams and Ken Ascher, this tender mother-child duet featured actual recordings of Helen Reddy's daughter Traci and reached #9 on the Hot 100, becoming one of her most personally beloved recordings.

1974.title: Rock and Roll Heaven
1974.fun_fact: A tribute to rock and roll musicians who had died young, including Jimi Hendrix, Janis Joplin, and Jim Morrison, this reached #3 on the Hot 100 and marked the Righteous Brothers' commercial comeback after several quiet years.

1974.title: Hollywood Swinging
1974.fun_fact: This funk celebration of Los Angeles culture reached #6 on the Hot 100 and demonstrated Kool and the Gang's ability to craft irresistibly danceable records before their later evolution into a more polished pop-R&B act.

1974.title: Be Thankful for What You Got
1974.fun_fact: Written and recorded entirely by William DeVaughn, a Washington D.C. architectural draftsman with no record label support, this self-financed soul recording reached #4 on the Hot 100 in an extraordinary independent success story.

1974.title: Hang on in There Baby
1974.fun_fact: Written and produced by Johnny Bristol, who had previously written Motown hits for Marvin Gaye and Tammi Terrell, this sensuous recording reached #8 on the Hot 100 and launched his performing career.

1974.title: Eres tú
1974.fun_fact: A Spanish pop group's entry in the Eurovision Song Contest, this romantic song sung in Spanish reached #9 on the Hot 100, one of the very few non-English language recordings to crack the US top 10.

1974.title: Takin' Care of Business
1974.fun_fact: Written in 15 minutes by Randy Bachman while waiting to go on stage, this Canadian hard rock anthem reached #12 on the Hot 100 and became the unofficial theme song of working-class rock radio.

1974.title: Radar Love
1974.fun_fact: Dutch rock band Golden Earring's hypnotic highway driving anthem reached #13 on the Hot 100 and is built on a distinctive piano riff that came to guitarist George Kooymans in a dream.

1974.title: Please Come to Boston
1974.fun_fact: Written by Dave Loggins about his actual experience asking a woman in Boston, Denver, and L.A. to join him, this charming folk-pop narrative reached #5 on the Hot 100 and was his only major hit.

1974.title: Keep on Smilin'
1974.fun_fact: This Southern rock band from Mobile, Alabama scored their biggest hit with this soulful rock anthem, which reached #10 on the Hot 100 and briefly suggested the group might achieve broader mainstream success.

1974.title: Lookin' for a Love
1974.fun_fact: Bobby Womack's cover of his own earlier Valentinos recording reached #10 on the Hot 100 and was part of his commercial comeback following years of struggle, showcasing his raw, emotionally direct guitar and vocal style.

1974.title: Put Your Hands Together
1974.fun_fact: Another Gamble and Huff production from Philadelphia International, this gospel-influenced soul track reached #10 on the Hot 100 and demonstrated the O'Jays' ability to deliver both social commentary and pure dance floor energy.

1974.title: On and On
1974.fun_fact: One of three Gladys Knight and the Pips songs in the 1974 year-end Hot 100, this reached #5 on the Hot 100 and highlighted their extraordinary commercial productivity in the immediate post-Motown years.

1974.title: Oh Very Young
1974.fun_fact: Cat Stevens wrote this meditation on mortality and the passage of youth for his Buddha and the Chocolate Box album, and it reached #10 on the Hot 100 in what would prove to be one of his final major US hits before his conversion to Islam.

1974.title: Leave Me Alone (Ruby Red Dress)
1974.fun_fact: Reaching #3 on the Hot 100, this was one of Helen Reddy's most successful hit singles, demonstrating the Australian-born singer's remarkable ability to deliver both feminist anthems and simple pop pleasures equally well.

1974.title: Goodbye Yellow Brick Road
1974.fun_fact: Bernie Taupin wrote these lyrics in a Caribbean hotel room in under an hour, and Elton John set them to music within minutes; the song reached #2 on the Hot 100 and has become one of the most played songs in pop history.

1974.title: (I've Been) Searchin' So Long
1974.fun_fact: Written by James Pankow as a deeply personal reflection on spiritual searching, this reached #9 on the Hot 100 and showcased a more introspective and philosophical side of Chicago's songwriting.

1974.title: Oh My My
1974.fun_fact: The second of two Ringo Starr songs in the 1974 year-end Hot 100, this reached #5 on the Hot 100 and featured contributions from several of his famous friends including Nicky Hopkins and Klaus Voormann.

1974.title: For the Love of Money
1974.fun_fact: Built on one of funk's most iconic bass riffs, played by Anthony Jackson, this Gamble and Huff production reached #9 on the Hot 100 and later became internationally famous as the theme song of The Apprentice television show.

1974.title: I Shot the Sheriff
1974.fun_fact: Eric Clapton's cover of Bob Marley's reggae track spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100 and was the key recording that introduced Bob Marley's songwriting to the American mainstream, dramatically boosting Marley's own career.

1974.title: Jet
1974.fun_fact: McCartney named this driving rock track after a Labrador puppy he had at his Scottish farm; it reached #7 on the Hot 100 and was one of the harder-rocking moments in his otherwise varied post-Beatles output.

1974.title: Don't Let the Sun Go Down on Me
1974.fun_fact: Written by Elton John and Bernie Taupin about Elton's feelings of creative exhaustion at the time, this reached #2 on the Hot 100 and featured background vocals from Carl Wilson and Bruce Johnston of the Beach Boys.

1974.title: Tubular Bells
1974.fun_fact: An excerpt from Mike Oldfield's debut album, which was the first release on Richard Branson's Virgin Records label, this reached #7 on the Hot 100 after being used as the main theme for the film The Exorcist.

1974.title: A Love Song
1974.fun_fact: Written by Kenny Loggins, this gentle country-pop ballad reached #12 on the Hot 100 and was part of Anne Murray's consistent run of melodically appealing hits that made her one of the decade's most reliable adult pop artists.

1974.title: I'm Leaving It (All) Up to You
1974.fun_fact: The first major duet from Donny and Marie Osmond reached #4 on the Hot 100 and established the sibling duo format that would anchor their long-running television variety show.

1974.title: Hello It's Me
1974.fun_fact: Originally recorded by Rundgren's band Nazz in 1969, this re-recorded solo version finally reached #5 on the Hot 100 five years later and became Todd Rundgren's biggest commercial hit as a performer.

1974.title: I Love
1974.fun_fact: Country songwriter Tom T. Hall's simple, charming list of life's small pleasures reached #12 on the Hot 100 in a rare crossover moment for an artist more typically associated with story songs on the country chart.

1974.title: Clap for the Wolfman
1974.artist: The Guess Who featuring Wolfman Jack
1974.fun_fact: Written as a tribute to the legendary DJ Wolfman Jack, who actually appeared on the recording, this reached #6 on the Hot 100 and was one of the final major chart entries for The Guess Who before their 1975 breakup.

1974.title: I'll Have to Say I Love You in a Song
1974.fun_fact: Released posthumously after Jim Croce's 1973 death, this charming acoustic ballad about being tongue-tied in an argument reached #9 on the Hot 100 and was based on a real incident with his wife Ingrid.

1974.title: The Lord's Prayer
1974.fun_fact: Australian nun Sister Janet Mead's rock arrangement of the Lord's Prayer reached #4 on the Hot 100 and earned a Grammy nomination, becoming the most commercially successful sacred music recording of the decade.

1974.title: Trying to Hold on to My Woman
1974.fun_fact: Holland-Dozier-Holland songwriter Lamont Dozier stepped from behind the writing desk to record this soulful track himself, reaching #15 on the Hot 100 as a solo artist.

1974.title: Don't You Worry 'bout a Thing
1974.fun_fact: One of Stevie Wonder's most joyful and Latin-influenced recordings from the Innervisions album, this reached #16 on the Hot 100 and showcased his growing interest in Afro-Cuban rhythms and worldly musical textures.

1974.title: A Very Special Love Song
1974.fun_fact: Charlie Rich's third entry in the 1974 year-end Hot 100 cemented his status as one of the year's biggest commercial forces, demonstrating the extraordinary reach of his pop-country crossover appeal.

1974.title: My Girl Bill
1974.fun_fact: This deliberately ambiguous story song reached #12 on the Hot 100 and briefly confused listeners about its subject before the twist ending revealed the narrative was more innocent than it initially sounded.

1974.title: Helen Wheels
1974.fun_fact: Named after a Land Rover McCartney owned in Scotland, this driving rock track reached #10 on the Hot 100 and was one of Wings' most energetic performances, demonstrating Paul McCartney's continued interest in pure rock and roll.

1974.title: My Mistake (Was to Love You)
1974.fun_fact: From their collaborative album Diana & Marvin, this duet reached #19 on the Hot 100 and was part of an inspired pairing of two of Motown's greatest voices that critics later recognized as criminally underappreciated.

1974.title: Wildwood Weed
1974.fun_fact: Jim Stafford's third entry in the 1974 year-end Hot 100 was this playful marijuana-themed novelty song that somehow reached #7 on the Hot 100 while cleverly coding its subject matter to slip past most radio programmers.

1974.title: Beach Baby
1974.fun_fact: A British studio group's loving tribute to the Beach Boys' early California sound reached #4 on the Hot 100 and was a perfectly realized exercise in pop nostalgia, capturing the essence of early 1960s surf music.

1974.title: Me and Baby Brother
1974.fun_fact: War's funky tribute to family bonds reached #15 on the Hot 100 and demonstrated the group's continuing commercial relevance during a period when they were one of the most innovative funk bands in American music.

1974.title: Rockin' Roll Baby
1974.fun_fact: Though slightly less successful than their earlier ballads, this Thom Bell production still reached #14 on the Hot 100 and showed the Stylistics' ability to work effectively in a more uptempo mode.

1974.title: I Honestly Love You
1974.fun_fact: Written by Jeff Barry and Peter Allen, this spare and emotionally direct ballad spent two weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 and won Olivia Newton-John Grammy Awards for both Record of the Year and Best Female Pop Vocal Performance.

1974.title: Call on Me
1974.fun_fact: Written by Lee Loughnane, this melodically rich track reached #6 on the Hot 100 and demonstrated Chicago's ability to write memorable hooks across a range of musical moods from jazz-rock to pure pop.

1974.title: Wild Thing
1974.fun_fact: British singer Helen Caunt, recording as Fancy, delivered a sultry, slowed-down cover of The Troggs' 1966 classic that reached #14 on the Hot 100 through sheer suggestive charm.

1974.title: Mighty Love
1974.fun_fact: Produced by Thom Bell, this two-part epic soul recording stretched the possibilities of what a pop single could be and reached #20 on the Hot 100, demonstrating Bell and the Spinners' artistic ambition alongside their commercial instincts.

1975.title: Love Will Keep Us Together
1975.fun_fact: Written by Neil Sedaka and Howard Greenfield, Toni Tennille and Daryl Dragon's cover spent four weeks at #1, sold over two million copies, and won the Grammy for Record of the Year, becoming one of the decade's defining pop recordings.

1975.title: Rhinestone Cowboy
1975.fun_fact: Written by Larry Weiss, Glen Campbell's arena-ready country crossover spent two weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 and became one of the most played radio songs of the decade, reviving his career at a crucial moment.

1975.title: Philadelphia Freedom
1975.fun_fact: Elton John wrote this as a personal gift for his friend Billie Jean King, whose World Team Tennis franchise was called the Philadelphia Freedoms; it spent two weeks at #1 on the Hot 100.

1975.title: Before the Next Teardrop Falls
1975.fun_fact: Tex-Mex musician Freddy Fender spent several years imprisoned before this soulful ballad spent two weeks at #1 on the Hot 100, representing one of pop music's most remarkable comeback stories.

1975.title: My Eyes Adored You
1975.fun_fact: Written by Bob Crewe and Kenny Nolan, this became Frankie Valli's first solo #1 hit apart from the Four Seasons and spent one week at the top of the Hot 100, demonstrating his commercial viability as a solo act.

1975.title: Some Kind of Wonderful
1975.fun_fact: Grand Funk Railroad's cover of the Carole King and Gerry Goffin-penned soul classic reached #3 on the Hot 100 and was a more polished, accessible recording than their earlier heavy rock material.

1975.title: Shining Star
1975.fun_fact: Written in one night before a recording session, this reached #1 on both the Hot 100 and R&B chart and won the Grammy for Best R&B Performance by a Duo or Group, becoming one of Earth Wind and Fire's signature recordings.

1975.title: Fame
1975.fun_fact: Co-written with John Lennon and Carlos Alomar during a chance studio session, this became David Bowie's only #1 single on the Hot 100 and marked a pivotal moment in his absorption of American funk and soul influences.

1975.title: Laughter in the Rain
1975.fun_fact: Neil Sedaka's comeback single after more than a decade away from the American charts spent three weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 and signaled the beginning of his remarkable artistic and commercial renaissance.

1975.title: One of These Nights
1975.fun_fact: Don Henley and Glenn Frey wrote this haunting track about searching for the perfect woman, and it spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100 and topped several other major charts, becoming one of the Eagles' most cinematic recordings.

1975.title: Thank God I'm a Country Boy
1975.fun_fact: Written by John Martin Sommers, John Denver's live version from his Greatest Hits album spent two weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 and crossed fully into the country chart, where it also reached #1.

1975.title: Jive Talkin'
1975.fun_fact: The Bee Gees wrote this after crossing a bridge in Miami that produced a rhythmic sound under their car tires; the track spent two weeks at #1 and marked their reinvention as a disco-influenced funk act.

1975.title: Best of My Love
1975.fun_fact: The first Eagles song to reach #1 on the Hot 100, this soft country-rock ballad written by Don Henley and Glenn Frey about a relationship in crisis spent one week at the top of the chart.

1975.title: Lovin' You
1975.fun_fact: Written by Minnie Riperton and her husband Richard Rudolph as a lullaby for their daughter Maya (later Maya Rudolph of Saturday Night Live), this spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100 and showcased Riperton's extraordinary five-octave vocal range.

1975.title: Kung Fu Fighting
1975.fun_fact: Carl Douglas wrote and recorded this in about ten minutes as a B-side filler, but it reached #1 on the Hot 100 and became one of the best-selling singles in UK history, capitalizing on the martial arts craze inspired by Bruce Lee films.

1975.title: Black Water
1975.fun_fact: The first Doobie Brothers song to reach #1 on the Hot 100, this acoustic New Orleans-flavored track was written by Pat Simmons and spent one week at the top of the chart, spending over a year on the singles chart overall.

1975.title: The Ballroom Blitz
1975.fun_fact: Written about an actual audience riot at a concert in Kilmarnock, Scotland, this glam rock classic reached #5 on the Hot 100 and has become one of the most recognizable opening riffs in rock history.

1975.title: (Hey Won't You Play) Another Somebody Done Somebody Wrong Song
1975.fun_fact: Written by Larry Butler and Chips Moman, this became B.J. Thomas's second #1 hit on the Hot 100 and one of the longest titles of any chart-topping single in history.

1975.title: He Don't Love You (Like I Love You)
1975.fun_fact: A cover of Jerry Butler's 1960 single, Tony Orlando and Dawn's version spent three weeks at #1 and was one of their final major hits before Tony Orlando's temporary retirement from the music industry in 1977.

1975.title: At Seventeen
1975.fun_fact: Janis Ian's devastating meditation on adolescent exclusion and social invisibility spent one week at #1 on the Adult Contemporary chart and reached #3 on the Hot 100, winning the Grammy for Best Female Pop Vocal Performance.

1975.title: Pick Up the Pieces
1975.fun_fact: A Scottish funk band recorded this infectious instrumental groove in New York, and it spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100, becoming one of the most sampled recordings of the 1970s era.

1975.title: The Hustle
1975.artist: Van McCoy & the Soul City Symphony
1975.fun_fact: Van McCoy was inspired to write this after watching people dance at a New York club; it spent three weeks at #1 on the Hot 100, popularized the Hustle dance, and became the defining sound of the emerging disco era.

1975.title: Lady Marmalade
1975.fun_fact: Written by Bob Crewe and Kenny Nolan and set in New Orleans' French Quarter, this spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100, making Labelle the first Black female group to appear on the cover of Rolling Stone magazine.

1975.title: Why Can't We Be Friends?
1975.fun_fact: Recorded while the Vietnam War was still a fresh wound, War's call for racial and international harmony reached #6 on the Hot 100 and was later broadcast into space during the 1975 Apollo-Soyuz test project joint mission.

1975.title: Love Won't Let Me Wait
1975.fun_fact: This sensuous Philadelphia soul ballad reached #5 on the Hot 100 and featured production by Bobby Eli that created an atmosphere of such intimacy some radio programmers briefly hesitated to add it to daytime rotations.

1975.title: Boogie On Reggae Woman
1975.fun_fact: Stevie Wonder's playful reggae-influenced funk track reached #3 on the Hot 100 and won the Grammy for Best Rhythm and Blues Song, demonstrating his remarkable range in the period of his most celebrated artistic output.

1975.title: Wasted Days and Wasted Nights
1975.fun_fact: Freddy Fender had originally recorded this in 1959 before his imprisonment; the new version released in the wake of 'Before the Next Teardrop Falls' reached #8 on the Hot 100 and extended his unexpected commercial renaissance.

1975.title: Angie Baby
1975.fun_fact: Written by Alan O'Day about a lonely girl who uses her imagination to make her life bearable, this surreal and somewhat unsettling pop song spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100 in late 1974.

1975.title: Fight the Power
1975.fun_fact: This politically charged funk epic reached #4 on the Hot 100 and became one of the most sampled recordings in hip-hop history when Public Enemy used its central groove as the foundation for their landmark 1989 track of the same name.

1975.title: Jackie Blue
1975.fun_fact: The Ozark Mountain Daredevils' only top 10 hit reached #3 on the Hot 100 with this atmospheric and slightly mysterious country-rock track that remains one of the most distinctive sounds from the mid-1970s AOR era.

1975.title: Fire
1975.fun_fact: The Ohio Players spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100 with this incendiary funk track and its accompanying album cover, featuring a model drenched in honey, became one of the decade's most talked-about pieces of album art.

1975.title: Magic
1975.fun_fact: Written by David Paton and Billy Lyall of the Scottish pop group Pilot, this reached #5 on the Hot 100 and was co-produced by Alan Parsons, who would later achieve fame with the Alan Parsons Project.

1975.title: Please Mr. Postman
1975.fun_fact: The Carpenters' faithful cover of The Marvelettes' original Motown classic spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100 and was one of their most successful recordings despite being a cover rather than original material.

1975.title: Sister Golden Hair
1975.fun_fact: Written by Gerry Beckley about his complicated feelings for a woman who wanted more commitment than he was ready to give, this spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100 and stands as one of America's most accomplished pop recordings.

1975.title: Lucy in the Sky with Diamonds
1975.fun_fact: Elton John's reggae-influenced cover of the Beatles classic featured John Lennon himself on rhythm guitar and backing vocals, making it one of the most distinguished Beatle collaborations of the post-breakup era.

1975.title: Mandy
1975.fun_fact: Originally titled 'Brandy' by Scott English, the title was changed to avoid confusion with Looking Glass's recent hit of the same name; Barry Manilow's version spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100 and launched his career as a major pop star.

1975.title: Have You Never Been Mellow
1975.fun_fact: Written by John Farrar, Olivia Newton-John's career producer, this gentle ballad spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100 and demonstrated the soft, reassuring pop style that made her one of the decade's most bankable artists.

1975.title: Could It Be Magic
1975.fun_fact: Based on Chopin's Prelude in C Minor, Barry Manilow's second entry in the 1975 year-end chart reached #6 on the Hot 100 and showcased his classical music training as foundation for his commercial pop style.

1975.title: Cat's in the Cradle
1975.fun_fact: Written with his wife Sandra about the fear that absentee fatherhood would be repeated through generations, this poignant folk song spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100 and remains Harry Chapin's most enduring recording.

1975.title: Wildfire
1975.fun_fact: Written by Michael Martin Murphey and Larry Cansler about a horse and young girl from Murphey's childhood memories in New Mexico, this mystical country-pop ballad reached #3 on the Hot 100.

1975.title: I'm Not Lisa
1975.fun_fact: Written by Jessi Colter, wife of outlaw country pioneer Waylon Jennings, about being compared unfavorably to a previous love, this reached #4 on the Hot 100 and was one of the few outlaw country recordings to cross over to mainstream pop.

1975.title: Listen to What the Man Said
1975.fun_fact: Paul McCartney's breezy pop track featured Dave Sanborn on saxophone and spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100; McCartney has said it is one of the songs he is most proud of for being unabashedly cheerful and simple.

1975.title: I'm Not in Love
1975.fun_fact: Built from a 48-track tape loop of vocal sounds recorded by the entire 10cc group, this elaborate production required 16 hours of recording to complete and spent one week at #2 on the Hot 100 while topping the UK chart.

1975.title: I Can Help
1975.fun_fact: Billy Swan wrote this irresistible rockabilly-flavored track in 20 minutes and recorded it with a tiny Farfisa organ riff that became one of 1974's most distinctive sounds; it reached #1 on the Hot 100.

1975.title: Fallin' in Love
1975.fun_fact: This soft pop group's second major hit reached #1 on the Hot 100 and was co-written by Denis Lambert and Brian Potter, the same team that had written Four Tops and Supremes hits for Motown in the 1960s.

1975.title: Feelings
1975.fun_fact: Brazilian singer Morris Albert's international ballad, which he wrote in French and English simultaneously at age 17, spent several weeks in the top 10 of the Hot 100 and became one of the most covered songs of the decade.

1975.title: When Will I Be Loved
1975.fun_fact: Linda Ronstadt's urgent cover of the Everly Brothers' 1960 original reached #2 on the Hot 100 and won the Grammy for Best Female Pop Vocal Performance, cementing her status as the queen of California rock.

1975.title: Chevy Van
1975.fun_fact: This ode to freedom and romantic adventure on the open road reached #5 on the Hot 100 and became a radio staple that perfectly captured the van culture and free-spirited ethos of mid-1970s America.

1975.title: You're the First, the Last, My Everything
1975.fun_fact: Barry White spent two weeks at #1 in the UK with this declaration of total devotion; it reached #2 on the Hot 100 and featured one of his most cinematic and grandiose orchestral arrangements.

1975.title: Please Mr. Please
1975.fun_fact: Olivia Newton-John's second entry in the 1975 year-end Hot 100 reached #3 on the Hot 100 and was a country-flavored request for a jukebox operator not to play a certain heartbreaking song.

1975.title: You're No Good
1975.fun_fact: Linda Ronstadt's fierce cover of Betty Everett's 1963 recording became her first #1 hit on the Hot 100, spending one week at the top, and the track's rock-country arrangement defined the emerging California sound.

1975.title: Dynomite
1975.fun_fact: This funk instrumental reached #10 on the Hot 100 and featured the explosive sounds of a bomb going off throughout the track, making it one of the more viscerally exciting funk recordings of the mid-1970s.

1975.title: Walking in Rhythm
1975.fun_fact: Donald Byrd's student jazz fusion group, the Blackbyrds, created this irresistibly groovy track that reached #6 on the Hot 100 and blended their jazz training with a dance floor sensibility that predated smooth jazz.

1975.title: The Way We Were/Try to Remember
1975.fun_fact: Gladys Knight's medley of two classic ballads reached #11 on the Hot 100 and was a remarkable artistic statement about memory and loss that showcased her interpretive gifts as one of soul music's great voices.

1975.title: Midnight Blue
1975.fun_fact: Written by Melissa Manchester and Carole Bayer Sager, this sophisticated pop ballad reached #6 on the Hot 100 and was the breakthrough hit that established Manchester as one of the more distinctive singer-songwriters of the era.

1975.title: Don't Call Us, We'll Call You
1975.fun_fact: Known for 'Green-Eyed Lady' in 1970, Sugarloaf returned with this satirical commentary on the music industry's treatment of artists, cleverly incorporating a phone call and radio static into its production and reaching #9 on the Hot 100.

1975.title: Poetry Man
1975.fun_fact: Phoebe Snow's jazz-influenced debut single reached #5 on the Hot 100 and introduced listeners to her extraordinary multi-octave voice and sophisticated songwriting sensibility, though she struggled to follow it with comparable commercial success.

1975.title: How Long?
1975.fun_fact: Paul Carrack's plaintive vocals on this British pub rock group's debut single about romantic betrayal reached #3 on the Hot 100; Carrack later became one of rock's most in-demand session vocalists.

1975.title: Express
1975.fun_fact: One of the first major disco hits to feature the phrase 'do it,' this Brooklyn soul collective's debut reached #4 on the Hot 100 and the extended album version became a New York club staple.

1975.title: That's the Way of the World
1975.fun_fact: The title track from the film of the same name, this earned Earth Wind and Fire their first Grammy nomination for Record of the Year and reached #12 on the Hot 100 from their most celebrated album.

1975.title: Lady
1975.fun_fact: Written by Dennis DeYoung about his wife Suzanne, this became Styx's commercial breakthrough after years of regional success, reaching #6 on the Hot 100 and introducing their arena rock sound to a national audience.

1975.title: Bad Time
1975.fun_fact: Grand Funk's power pop turn reached #4 on the Hot 100 and showed the group could write in the same melodic style as their contemporaries, though it marked one of their final significant chart entries.

1975.title: Only Women Bleed
1975.fun_fact: Alice Cooper's thoughtful ballad about domestic abuse represented a surprising departure from his theatrical shock rock image and reached #12 on the Hot 100, showing considerable artistic range.

1975.title: Doctor's Orders
1975.fun_fact: Written by Roger Cook, Roger Greenaway, and Geoff Stephens, this Brooklyn singer's disco track reached #11 on the Hot 100 and was one of the early crossover disco recordings to succeed on mainstream pop radio.

1975.title: Get Down Tonight
1975.fun_fact: Harry Wayne Casey and Richard Finch wrote this in five minutes during a recording session; it spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100 and was one of the foundational recordings of the Miami-based disco sound.

1975.title: One Man Woman/One Woman Man
1975.fun_fact: Paul Anka's declaration of marital fidelity, recorded as a duet with Odia Coates, reached #7 on the Hot 100 at a time when open marriages and sexual liberation were major cultural talking points.

1975.title: You Are So Beautiful
1975.fun_fact: Written by Billy Preston and Bruce Fisher, Joe Cocker's trembling, emotionally raw interpretation reached #5 on the Hot 100 and has since become one of the most-performed wedding songs of the late 20th century.

1975.title: Feel Like Makin' Love
1975.fun_fact: Paul Rodgers' first post-Free band reached #10 on the Hot 100 with this smoldering ballad, and the song demonstrated that the raw emotional power of his voice translated effectively from hard rock to quiet introspection.

1975.title: How Sweet It Is (To Be Loved by You)
1975.fun_fact: James Taylor's laid-back cover of Marvin Gaye's 1964 Motown classic reached #5 on the Hot 100 and is one of the most successful examples of a white artist bringing a new sensibility to Motown material.

1975.title: Dance with Me
1975.fun_fact: Written by John Hall and Johanna Hall of the band Orleans, this gentle pop track reached #6 on the Hot 100 and John Hall would later use his music industry success to launch a political career, serving in the U.S. Congress.

1975.title: Cut the Cake
1975.fun_fact: The Average White Band's second major US hit reached #10 on the Hot 100 and demonstrated that their Scottish funk credentials were no fluke, confirming their ability to craft multiple strong recordings in the American R&B style.

1975.title: Never Can Say Goodbye
1975.fun_fact: Gloria Gaynor's extended disco remix of the Jackson 5's 1971 hit is generally credited as one of the first 12-inch dance singles, a format that would revolutionize club music and become the standard for disco and eventually house music.

1975.title: I Don't Like to Sleep Alone
1975.fun_fact: The second Paul Anka and Odia Coates duet in the 1975 year-end Hot 100, this reached #8 on the Hot 100 and made the duo one of the year's more successful recording partnerships.

1975.title: Morning Side of the Mountain
1975.fun_fact: A cover of Tommy Edwards's 1951 recording, Donny and Marie Osmond's version reached #8 on the Hot 100 and became a fan favorite during their television variety show's regular performances.

1975.title: When Will I See You Again
1975.fun_fact: This Philadelphia International Records trio's bittersweet farewell song reached #2 on the Hot 100 and was a massive global hit, spending several weeks at #1 in the UK and numerous other countries.

1975.title: Get Down, Get Down (Get on the Floor)
1975.fun_fact: Joe Simon's funk crossover reached #8 on the Hot 100 and was a late-career hit that demonstrated his flexibility in adapting to the evolving dance music landscape of the mid-1970s.

1975.title: I'm Sorry
1975.fun_fact: John Denver's achingly sincere ballad about failing to be present in a relationship spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100 and reportedly drew from the tensions in his own marriage to Annie Adams Denver.

1975.title: Killer Queen
1975.fun_fact: Freddie Mercury wrote this character study of a high-class call girl as a deliberate attempt to write a pop hit in the style of Noël Coward; it reached #12 on the Hot 100 and became Queen's first major US chart entry.

1975.title: Shoeshine Boy
1975.fun_fact: Former Temptation Eddie Kendricks continued his disco-influenced solo career with this track, which reached #18 on the Hot 100 during his extraordinary run of consistent chart success following his departure from the group.

1975.title: Do It ('Til You're Satisfied)
1975.fun_fact: B.T. Express's second major hit reached #2 on the Hot 100 and was one of the foundational recordings of the Brooklyn-based funk and disco scene that would later evolve into the hip-hop movement.

1975.title: Can't Get It Out of My Head
1975.fun_fact: Jeff Lynne's orchestral rock epic reached #9 on the Hot 100 and was the breakthrough moment that established ELO as a major commercial force in America, finally fulfilling the promise of Lynne's ambitious Beatles-meets-Beethoven vision.

1975.title: Sha-La-La (Make Me Happy)
1975.fun_fact: Al Green's joyful pop-soul hybrid reached #7 on the Hot 100 and was one of his final major pop hits before he devoted himself entirely to gospel music following a traumatic personal incident in 1974.

1975.title: Lonely People
1975.fun_fact: Written as a response to The Beatles' 'Eleanor Rigby' and its theme of isolation, this reached #5 on the Hot 100 and was one of America's most thoughtful and melodically rich recordings.

1975.title: You Got the Love
1975.fun_fact: Chaka Khan's explosive vocal performance on this Rufus track reached #11 on the Hot 100 and further built her reputation as one of the most powerful voices in contemporary soul and funk music.

1975.title: The Rockford Files Theme
1975.fun_fact: Television composer Mike Post's funky instrumental theme from the popular NBC detective series reached #10 on the Hot 100 in one of the more unlikely crossover moments of the mid-1970s.

1975.title: It Only Takes a Minute
1975.fun_fact: The Tavares brothers' energetic vocal group scored their breakthrough hit with this track that reached #10 on the Hot 100, though it is better known outside the US through the UK hit version recorded by Take That in 1992.

1975.title: No No Song
1975.fun_fact: Hoyt Axton's comic song about turning down drugs was delivered with great charm by Ringo Starr, who reached #3 on the Hot 100 with this good-natured rejection of the counterculture excesses he had witnessed as a Beatle.

1975.title: Junior's Farm
1975.fun_fact: Named after a real farm in Tennessee owned by Wings roadie Junior Giscombe, this energetic rocker reached #3 on the Hot 100 and was one of McCartney's most spontaneous-sounding recordings of the decade.

1975.title: Bungle in the Jungle
1975.fun_fact: Ian Anderson's meditation on the predatory nature of urban business life reached #12 on the Hot 100 and remains one of the more unusual entries in Jethro Tull's catalog, lighter and more accessible than their extended progressive rock suites.

1975.title: Long Tall Glasses (I Can Dance)
1975.fun_fact: Written by Leo Sayer and David Courtney, this tale of a reluctant dancer who discovers unexpected rhythm reached #9 on the Hot 100 and introduced the British performer to American audiences.

1975.title: Misty
1975.fun_fact: Ray Stevens's smooth pop cover of Erroll Garner's jazz standard reached #14 on the Hot 100 and demonstrated his versatility far beyond the novelty songs for which he was most commercially known.

1975.title: Someone Saved My Life Tonight
1975.fun_fact: Written about Elton John being talked out of a suicide attempt by longtime friend Long John Baldry in the mid-1960s, this reached #4 on the Hot 100 and is one of the most personally revealing songs in the Elton John and Bernie Taupin catalog.

1975.title: Bad Blood
1975.fun_fact: Neil Sedaka's second #1 of 1975 featured backing vocals by Elton John, who had championed Sedaka's US comeback on his Rocket Records label, and it spent three weeks at the top of the Hot 100.

1975.title: Only Yesterday
1975.fun_fact: Written by Richard Carpenter and John Bettis, this reached #4 on the Hot 100 and featured the kind of flawless studio production that made Carpenters recordings sound simultaneously effortless and meticulously crafted.

1975.title: I'm on Fire
1975.fun_fact: This Tulsa-based power pop duo's debut single reached #16 on the Hot 100 and was strongly influenced by early Beatles recordings, featuring a raw energy that impressed critics but never translated into consistent commercial success.

1975.title: Only You (And You Alone)
1975.fun_fact: Ringo Starr's touching cover of the Platters' 1955 doo-wop classic reached #6 on the Hot 100 and was one of his most commercially successful single recordings of the post-Beatles decade.

1975.title: Third Rate Romance
1975.fun_fact: Written by Russell Smith about a mundane motel room encounter between two strangers, this wry country-rock narrative reached #14 on the Hot 100 for this Memphis-based group.

1975.title: You Ain't Seen Nothing Yet
1975.fun_fact: Randy Bachman originally recorded this stuttering hard rock track as a joke for his brother Gary, who had a stutter; when the band's producer insisted it be a serious release, it spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100.

1975.title: Swearin' to God
1975.fun_fact: Frankie Valli's energetic disco-flavored track reached #6 on the Hot 100 and was one of several successful singles he released in 1975 as he continued to build his solo career alongside his work with the Four Seasons.

1975.title: Get Dancin'
1975.fun_fact: Bob Crewe's theatrical disco concept project reached #10 on the Hot 100 with this campy, high-energy track that perfectly captured the anything-goes spirit of the early disco era before it became more formulaic.

1976.title: Silly Love Songs
1976.fun_fact: Paul McCartney wrote this in direct response to critics who dismissed his post-Beatles work as lightweight; it spent five weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 and became one of the best-selling singles of the entire decade.

1976.title: Don't Go Breaking My Heart
1976.fun_fact: Recorded under the pseudonyms Ann Orson and Carte Blanche, this playful duet spent four weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 and was Elton John's first UK #1 hit, a remarkable gap given his global superstar status.

1976.title: Disco Lady
1976.fun_fact: The first single certified platinum by the RIAA under their new certification rules, this spent four weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 and was produced by Don Davis in a sleek Philly-influenced disco style.

1976.title: December, 1963 (Oh, What a Night)
1976.fun_fact: Originally written about Prohibition-era America but changed to a story of a young man's first romantic encounter, this spent three weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 and was the Four Seasons' first major hit in nearly a decade.

1976.title: Play That Funky Music
1976.fun_fact: Rob Parissi wrote this after an audience member shouted 'play that funky music, white boy' at his band's show; it spent three weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 and was the only major hit for Wild Cherry.

1976.title: Kiss and Say Goodbye
1976.fun_fact: Written by Winfred Lovett and featuring his spoken word introduction, this smooth soul farewell song spent two weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 and became one of the definitive recordings of the vocal group soul era.

1976.title: Love Machine
1976.fun_fact: The first Miracles hit without Smokey Robinson spent two weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 and proved the group could achieve major commercial success after their most famous member's departure.

1976.title: 50 Ways to Leave Your Lover
1976.fun_fact: Built around Steve Gadd's distinctive drum pattern that Paul Simon heard during a sound check, this spent three weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 and features one of the most memorable chorus constructions in pop music history.

1976.title: Love Is Alive
1976.fun_fact: Gary Wright pioneered an all-synthesizer backing track for this recording, using no guitars or bass, and it reached #2 on the Hot 100 as one of the most commercially successful applications of synthesizer technology in rock music to that point.

1976.title: A Fifth of Beethoven
1976.artist: Walter Murphy & The Big Apple Band
1976.fun_fact: Walter Murphy's disco adaptation of Beethoven's Fifth Symphony spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100 and was featured in the film Saturday Night Fever, exposing it to an even wider audience than its initial chart run.

1976.title: Sara Smile
1976.fun_fact: Written by Daryl Hall as a love song for his then-girlfriend Sara Allen, who later became his long-term partner, this spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100 and launched Hall and Oates into mainstream stardom.

1976.title: Afternoon Delight
1976.fun_fact: Written by Bill Danoff about brunch at a Washington D.C. restaurant and a subsequent romantic daydream, this spent two weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 and won the Grammy for Best New Artist despite its overt double meaning being debated.

1976.title: I Write the Songs
1976.fun_fact: Written by Bruce Johnston of the Beach Boys and intended as a song sung from the perspective of music itself as a universal force, Barry Manilow's version spent one week at #1 and won the Grammy for Song of the Year.

1976.title: Fly, Robin, Fly
1976.fun_fact: A German disco trio's record spent three weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 despite having lyrics consisting almost entirely of the title phrase, winning the Grammy for Best R&B Performance by a Duo or Group in a surprising upset.

1976.title: Love Hangover
1976.fun_fact: Diana Ross recorded this seductive disco track in a single late-night session after producer Hal Davis played her the backing track; it spent two weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 and marked her full embrace of the disco era.

1976.title: Get Closer
1976.fun_fact: The folk-pop duo's biggest hit since 'Summer Breeze' reached #6 on the Hot 100 and featured a warm, inviting production that contrasted with the harder-edged rock dominating FM radio at the time.

1976.title: More, More, More
1976.fun_fact: Andrea True, a former adult film actress, recorded this disco track in Jamaica to bypass immigration issues and reached #4 on the Hot 100; the song's repetitive hook became one of the era's most recognizable disco phrases.

1976.title: Bohemian Rhapsody
1976.fun_fact: Freddie Mercury's six-minute operatic rock suite was considered completely uncommercial by label executives until DJ Kenny Everett played it on British radio and the public demand forced its release; it reached #9 on the Hot 100.

1976.title: Misty Blue
1976.fun_fact: Dorothy Moore's deeply emotional cover of this Bob Montgomery composition spent one week at #1 on the R&B chart and reached #3 on the Hot 100, becoming one of the most affecting soul ballads of 1976.

1976.title: Boogie Fever
1976.fun_fact: Produced by Freddie Perren, this spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100 and was the biggest hit for the Los Angeles sibling group, featuring the kind of polished, family-friendly soul-pop that defined their sound.

1976.title: I'd Really Love to See You Tonight
1976.fun_fact: Written by Parker McGee, this gentle late-night longing ballad reached #2 on the Hot 100 and launched the commercial career of this Texas soft rock duo, who would place several more hits in the top 10.

1976.title: You Sexy Thing
1976.fun_fact: Written by Errol Brown, this British soul group's most enduring recording reached #3 on the Hot 100 and has since appeared in countless films and television programs, most famously The Full Monty.

1976.title: Love Hurts
1976.fun_fact: A cover of the Everly Brothers' 1960 recording, Nazareth's heavy rock interpretation reached #8 on the Hot 100 and became one of the defining power ballads of the hard rock era.

1976.title: Get Up and Boogie
1976.fun_fact: Silver Convention's second major US hit reached #4 on the Hot 100 and confirmed that their formula of minimal lyrics over a propulsive disco groove had genuine commercial longevity beyond their initial success.

1976.title: Take It to the Limit
1976.fun_fact: Written primarily by bassist Randy Meisner as a meditation on ambition and its personal costs, this reached #4 on the Hot 100 and featured Meisner's falsetto on the climactic high notes that became the song's emotional centerpiece.

1976.title: (Shake, Shake, Shake) Shake Your Booty
1976.fun_fact: KC and the Sunshine Band's second #1 hit of 1976 spent one week at the top of the Hot 100 and was part of an extraordinary run of dance floor dominance that made them one of the era's most commercially potent acts.

1976.title: Sweet Love
1976.fun_fact: Before Lionel Richie's ballad-focused solo career, the Commodores delivered this smooth soul track that reached #5 on the Hot 100 and showcased the group's range beyond their harder funk recordings.

1976.title: Right Back Where We Started From
1976.fun_fact: British singer Maxine Nightingale's uplifting pop track reached #8 on the Hot 100 and has since achieved a second life through appearances in films including Lifeforce and Notting Hill.

1976.title: Theme from S.W.A.T.
1976.fun_fact: Produced by Steve Barri and Michael Omartian, this television theme adaptation spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100 and was part of a mid-1970s trend of TV theme songs achieving mainstream pop chart success.

1976.title: Love Rollercoaster
1976.fun_fact: The Ohio Players spent one week at #1 with this funk classic, and a persistent urban legend claimed that a woman's scream audible in the recording was an actual murder victim, a story the band has always denied.

1976.title: You Should Be Dancing
1976.fun_fact: The Bee Gees spent one week at #1 with this fully realized disco track, signaling their complete transformation from soft rock balladeers into the architects of the disco era that would dominate their work on Saturday Night Fever.

1976.title: You'll Never Find Another Love Like Mine
1976.fun_fact: Produced by Kenny Gamble and Leon Huff at Philadelphia International Records, this spent two weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 and was the biggest commercial moment in Lou Rawls's career despite decades of consistent recording.

1976.title: Golden Years
1976.fun_fact: David Bowie's disco-flavored track was reportedly written as a potential gift for Elvis Presley, who declined it; it reached #10 on the Hot 100 and was from the Station to Station album, one of his most critically acclaimed works.

1976.title: Moonlight Feels Right
1976.fun_fact: This Atlanta pop group's only major hit featured a marimba solo that became one of the more distinctive instrumental moments of 1976, and the track reached #3 on the Hot 100.

1976.title: Only Sixteen
1976.fun_fact: Dr. Hook's cover of Sam Cooke's 1959 coming-of-age song reached #6 on the Hot 100 and was part of their successful strategy of revisiting classic pop material with their own warm and somewhat self-deprecating vocal style.

1976.title: Let Your Love Flow
1976.fun_fact: Written by David Bellamy after a friend suggested he try writing a simple folk song, this spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100 and made the Bellamy Brothers the only new act to score a #1 pop hit in the spring of 1976.

1976.title: Dream Weaver
1976.fun_fact: Written during a meditation session inspired by the writings of Paramahansa Yogananda, Gary Wright recorded this with all synthesizers and no conventional rock instruments, reaching #2 on the Hot 100.

1976.title: Turn the Beat Around
1976.fun_fact: Written by Gerald Jackson and Peter Jackson Jr., this disco anthem reached #10 on the Hot 100 and became a club staple whose endurance was confirmed when Gloria Estefan's 1994 cover version also became a top 10 hit.

1976.title: Lonely Night (Angel Face)
1976.fun_fact: Neil Sedaka wrote this track specifically for Captain and Tennille, and it reached #3 on the Hot 100 as part of their run of consistent hits following the massive success of 'Love Will Keep Us Together.'

1976.title: All by Myself
1976.fun_fact: Based on Sergei Rachmaninoff's Piano Concerto No. 2, Eric Carmen forgot to obtain permission to use the classical theme and had to pay royalties retroactively; the song reached #2 on the Hot 100.

1976.title: Love to Love You Baby
1976.fun_fact: Producer Giorgio Moroder asked Donna Summer to sing as if she were making love and improvise; the 17-minute album version created such controversy that some stations banned it, but the edited single reached #2 on the Hot 100.

1976.title: Deep Purple
1976.fun_fact: Donny and Marie Osmond's duet cover of the 1963 Nino Tempo and April Stevens original reached #14 on the Hot 100 and was one of several charming pop covers the siblings recorded for their variety television series.

1976.title: Theme from Mahogany (Do You Know Where You're Going To)
1976.fun_fact: From the Diana Ross film Mahogany, this became one of her signature recordings, spending one week at #1 on the Hot 100 and earning an Academy Award nomination for Best Original Song.

1976.title: Sweet Thing
1976.fun_fact: Chaka Khan's vocal performance on this Rufus track reached #5 on the Hot 100 and was one of the last major Rufus hits before Khan fully transitioned her focus to her solo career.

1976.title: That's the Way (I Like It)
1976.fun_fact: KC and the Sunshine Band's third major hit of the year spent two weeks at #1 on the Hot 100, making them one of the most commercially dominant acts of 1976 alongside the Bee Gees and Elton John.

1976.title: A Little Bit More
1976.fun_fact: Written by Bobby Gosh, this became Dr. Hook's first UK #1 hit and reached #11 on the Hot 100, becoming one of their most successful ballads during a period when they were one of AM radio's most consistent acts.

1976.title: Shannon
1976.fun_fact: Written as a tribute to Carl Wilson's (of The Beach Boys) deceased Irish Setter dog named Shannon, this melancholy pop ballad reached #6 on the Hot 100 and became Henry Gross's only significant hit.

1976.title: If You Leave Me Now
1976.fun_fact: This marked Chicago's musical evolution from brass-rock toward soft adult contemporary, spent two weeks at #1 on the Hot 100, and won Grammy Awards for both Best Pop Vocal Performance by a Duo or Group and Best Arrangement.

1976.title: Lowdown
1976.fun_fact: Co-written by Boz Scaggs and David Paich, who later founded Toto, this smooth jazz-funk track reached #3 on the Hot 100 and won the Grammy for Best Rhythm and Blues Song, defining the blue-eyed soul sound.

1976.title: Show Me the Way
1976.fun_fact: Recorded live at the Winterland Ballroom in San Francisco, Peter Frampton's talk box guitar showcase reached #6 on the Hot 100 and was part of Frampton Comes Alive, which became one of the best-selling live albums of all time.

1976.title: Dream On
1976.fun_fact: Originally released in 1973 with minimal commercial success, Aerosmith re-released this with a new arrangement in 1976 and it reached #6 on the Hot 100, launching their period of mainstream commercial breakthrough.

1976.title: I Love Music
1976.fun_fact: Kenny Gamble and Leon Huff wrote this self-referential celebration of music's unifying power for the O'Jays, and it reached #5 on the Hot 100 and #4 on the R&B chart.

1976.title: Say You Love Me
