1976.fun_fact: Christine McVie wrote this after a painful breakup, and it became one of the standout tracks from the Rumours sessions that would eventually yield one of the best-selling albums in history, reaching #11 on the Hot 100.

1976.title: Times of Your Life
1976.fun_fact: Originally written for a Kodak advertising campaign, Paul Anka transformed this jingle into a full pop ballad that reached #7 on the Hot 100 and became one of the most effective examples of commercial music crossing over to genuine emotional resonance.

1976.title: Devil Woman
1976.fun_fact: This dark, mysterious single became Cliff Richard's first major US hit after nearly two decades of stardom in the UK, reaching #6 on the Hot 100 and introducing American audiences to one of Britain's most enduring pop performers.

1976.title: Fooled Around and Fell in Love
1976.fun_fact: Featuring lead vocals by Mickey Thomas (later of Starship), not Elvin Bishop himself, this reached #3 on the Hot 100 and was the career commercial peak for Bishop, a founding member of the Paul Butterfield Blues Band.

1976.title: Convoy
1976.fun_fact: Written to cash in on the Citizens Band radio craze sweeping America, this truckers' anthem spent six weeks at #1 on the Hot 100, inspired the 1978 Kris Kristofferson film of the same name, and helped popularize CB radio slang in everyday speech.

1976.title: Welcome Back
1976.fun_fact: Written and recorded in two weeks for the television series Welcome Back, Kotter, John Sebastian's theme song spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100 and gave the former Lovin' Spoonful frontman his most successful solo recording.

1976.title: Sing a Song
1976.fun_fact: This joyful Earth, Wind and Fire track reached #5 on the Hot 100 and was part of their Spirit album, which confirmed their status as one of the 1970s' most musically sophisticated and consistently innovative funk-soul groups.

1976.title: Heaven Must Be Missing an Angel
1976.fun_fact: Produced by Freddie Perren, this disco-inflected soul track reached #15 on the Hot 100 and became a staple of the nascent disco scene in clubs well before the genre fully crossed over to mainstream radio.

1976.title: I'll Be Good to You
1976.fun_fact: Produced by Quincy Jones, this was the debut single for the Johnson brothers and reached #3 on the Hot 100, beginning a productive partnership with Jones that would yield several major hits over the following years.

1976.title: Rock and Roll Music
1976.fun_fact: The Beach Boys' cover of Chuck Berry's 1957 original reached #5 on the Hot 100, giving them their biggest hit in years despite being a straightforward nostalgic gesture rather than an innovative artistic statement.

1976.title: Shop Around
1976.fun_fact: Captain and Tennille's update of Smokey Robinson and the Miracles' 1960 Motown classic reached #4 on the Hot 100 and was part of their strategic approach of mining pop history for familiar material to reinterpret for contemporary audiences.

1976.title: Saturday Night
1976.fun_fact: Scottish teen idols Bay City Rollers spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100 with this tartan-flavored bubblegum pop track, triggering a level of teen mania in America that briefly rivaled the original Beatlemania of 1964.

1976.title: Island Girl
1976.fun_fact: Elton John's second #1 of 1975 (reaching the chart position in late 1975 to appear on the 1976 year-end chart) spent three weeks at the top of the Hot 100 and featured Bernie Taupin's most directly observed character portrait.

1976.title: Let's Do It Again
1976.fun_fact: From Curtis Mayfield's film of the same name, this reached #1 on the Hot 100 and won the Grammy for Best Rhythm and Blues Song, giving the Staple Singers a second chart-topper after 'I'll Take You There.'

1976.title: Let 'Em In
1976.fun_fact: Paul McCartney's second entry in the 1976 year-end Hot 100 featured the names of family members and friends being announced at a front door, reached #3 on the Hot 100, and became one of Wings' most warmly received recordings.

1976.title: Baby Face
1976.artist: Wing and a Prayer Fife and Drum Corps
1976.fun_fact: A disco update of the 1926 Tin Pan Alley classic, this studio group's recording reached #14 on the Hot 100 and was part of a wave of nostalgic pop revivals that capitalized on America's bicentennial spirit.

1976.title: This Masquerade
1976.fun_fact: Written by Leon Russell and featuring George Benson's simultaneous guitar and vocal performance in unison, this jazz-pop crossover spent one week at #1 and won the Grammy for Record of the Year, the first jazz record to do so.

1976.title: Evil Woman
1976.fun_fact: Jeff Lynne wrote this bitter breakup song in about 15 minutes and it reached #10 on the Hot 100, marking ELO's commercial arrival in the United States as a major singles act rather than purely an album-oriented band.

1976.title: Wham Bam
1976.fun_fact: Boz Scaggs associate John Hewitt recorded this under the name Silver, and the track reached #16 on the Hot 100 with its polished California sound.

1976.title: I'm Easy
1976.fun_fact: Keith Carradine wrote and performed this intimate folk song for Robert Altman's film Nashville, performing it in character in an unforgettable scene; it won the Academy Award for Best Original Song and reached #17 on the Hot 100.

1976.title: Wake Up Everybody
1976.fun_fact: A social conscience anthem produced by Gamble and Huff, featuring Teddy Pendergrass on lead vocals, this reached #12 on the Hot 100 and was one of the final major hits for this lineup before Pendergrass launched his solo career.

1976.title: Summer
1976.fun_fact: War's nostalgic celebration of summer love and Los Angeles street life reached #7 on the Hot 100 and demonstrated their continued commercial relevance even as the group's membership underwent gradual changes.

1976.title: Let Her In
1976.fun_fact: Before his film breakthrough with Saturday Night Fever and Grease, John Travolta scored a top 10 pop hit with this track that reached #10 on the Hot 100 based primarily on the teen audience following his Welcome Back Kotter television success.

1976.title: Fox on the Run
1976.fun_fact: Written by the entire Sweet band after they decided to prove they could write their own hits rather than relying on Chinn and Chapman, this reached #5 on the Hot 100 and was one of glam rock's final major commercial statements.

1976.title: Rhiannon
1976.fun_fact: Stevie Nicks wrote this about a Welsh witch from a paperback novel she was reading on a tour bus, and the live performances of this song, where Nicks entered a trance-like state, became legendary among Fleetwood Mac audiences.

1976.title: Got to Get You into My Life
1976.fun_fact: Originally released in 1966 on the Revolver album, Paul McCartney's soul-influenced Beatle recording charted again in 1976 after being released as a single from the Rock 'n' Roll Music compilation, reaching #7 on the Hot 100.

1976.title: Fanny (Be Tender with My Love)
1976.fun_fact: One of the Bee Gees' transitional recordings between their earlier ballad style and their full disco era, this reached #12 on the Hot 100 and showcased Barry Gibb's falsetto becoming the dominant vocal element in their sound.

1976.title: Getaway
1976.fun_fact: Another Earth, Wind and Fire funk gem that reached #12 on the Hot 100, this track exemplified the group's ability to blend sophisticated musicianship with genuinely infectious dance floor energy.

1976.title: She's Gone
1976.fun_fact: Originally released in 1974 without significant success, this brooding blue-eyed soul track was re-released after the success of 'Sara Smile' and reached #7 on the Hot 100, revealing the depth of Hall and Oates's back catalog.

1976.title: Still the One
1976.fun_fact: John Hall and Johanna Hall wrote this as a wedding anniversary gift to each other, and it reached #5 on the Hot 100; the song has since been adopted by several politicians as a campaign theme, most notably George W. Bush.

1976.title: You're My Best Friend
1976.fun_fact: Written by bassist John Deacon for his wife Veronica on a Wurlitzer electric piano, this reached #16 on the Hot 100 and was one of the few Queen hits not dominated by Freddie Mercury's compositional voice.

1976.title: With Your Love
1976.fun_fact: Grace Slick's vocal performance on this Jefferson Starship ballad reached #12 on the Hot 100 and demonstrated the band's commercial adaptability as they shifted away from the psychedelic rock of their Jefferson Airplane predecessor.

1976.title: Slow Ride
1976.fun_fact: Edited from an eight-minute album track to a more radio-friendly length, Foghat's bluesy boogie rock anthem reached #20 on the Hot 100 and became one of the most played songs on album-oriented FM radio for years.

1976.title: Who'd She Coo?
1976.fun_fact: One of the final major hits for the Ohio Players in their classic lineup, this funk track reached #18 on the Hot 100 as the group's commercial momentum began to slow after their peak years of 1974-1975.

1976.title: The Boys Are Back in Town
1976.fun_fact: Phil Lynott wrote this celebration of his old friends in Dublin, and it reached #12 on the Hot 100, becoming one of hard rock's most anthemic recordings and introducing Thin Lizzy's twin-guitar attack to American audiences.

1976.title: Walk Away from Love
1976.fun_fact: Former Temptations lead singer David Ruffin scored his biggest solo hit with this Barry Goldberg production, reaching #9 on the Hot 100 in a long-overdue commercial breakthrough for one of soul music's most distinctive voices.

1976.title: Baby, I Love Your Way (Live)
1976.fun_fact: The second Frampton Comes Alive hit in the 1976 year-end chart, this live version of his tender acoustic ballad reached #12 on the Hot 100 and the parent album eventually sold over eight million copies in the United States alone.

1976.title: Young Hearts Run Free
1976.fun_fact: Written and produced by Dave Crawford as a warning to other women about unfaithful partners, based on Candi Staton's own experiences in a difficult marriage, this disco-soul track reached #20 on the Hot 100.

1976.title: Breaking Up Is Hard to Do
1976.fun_fact: Neil Sedaka re-recorded his own 1962 #1 hit as a slow ballad in 1975 and this new version spent one week at #1 on the Adult Contemporary chart and reached #8 on the Hot 100, making him one of the only artists to take the same song to the top in two different decades.

1976.title: Money Honey
1976.fun_fact: The Bay City Rollers' second entry in the 1976 year-end Hot 100 reached #9 on the Hot 100 and confirmed their status as the decade's most successful pop teen idol group since the Osmonds.

1976.title: Give Up the Funk (Tear the Roof off the Sucker)
1976.fun_fact: George Clinton's Parliament collective reached #15 on the Hot 100 with this funk manifesto, and the recording has since become one of the most heavily sampled tracks in hip-hop history, appearing in over 700 recorded songs.

1976.title: Junk Food Junkie
1976.fun_fact: This comedic folk song about a health food devotee who secretly craves junk food reached #9 on the Hot 100 and was a one-hit wonder that captured the nation's nascent obsession with natural foods and the counterculture of health eating.

1976.title: Tryin' to Get the Feeling Again
1976.fun_fact: Written by David Pomerantz, this became one of Barry Manilow's most emotionally resonant recordings and reached #10 on the Hot 100 as part of his sustained commercial dominance of adult contemporary radio.

1976.title: Rock and Roll All Nite (Live)
1976.fun_fact: Kiss's live recording from Alive! reached #12 on the Hot 100 and helped transform the album into one of the decade's best-selling live records, saving the band's career at a critical commercial moment.

1976.title: Disco Duck
1976.artist: Rick Dees & His Cast of Idiots
1976.fun_fact: Memphis DJ Rick Dees recorded this novelty parody of disco culture as a joke and it spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100, becoming both a celebration and gentle mockery of the disco craze simultaneously.

1976.title: Take the Money and Run
1976.fun_fact: Steve Miller's tale of fictional criminals Billy Joe and Bobby Sue reached #11 on the Hot 100 and was part of his most commercially productive period, demonstrating his knack for crafting irresistibly singable pop-rock narratives.

1976.title: Squeeze Box
1976.fun_fact: Pete Townshend's surprisingly cheerful accordion-based double entendre reached #16 on the Hot 100 and was a deliberate attempt to write something uncomplicated and fun after the conceptual ambitions of Tommy and Quadrophenia.

1976.title: Country Boy (You Got Your Feet in L.A.)
1976.fun_fact: Written by Dennis Lambert and Brian Potter, this celebration of retaining rural values amid Hollywood glitz reached #11 on the Hot 100 and was part of Glen Campbell's continued ability to straddle pop and country radio effortlessly.

1977.title: Tonight's the Night (Gonna Be Alright)
1977.fun_fact: Rod Stewart's seduction ballad spent eight weeks at #1 on the Hot 100, the longest run at #1 of any single in 1977, and was banned by several radio stations for its frank sexual narrative.

1977.title: I Just Want to Be Your Everything
1977.fun_fact: Barry Gibb wrote this for his youngest brother Andy, who became the first solo artist to have his first three singles all reach #1 on the Hot 100, a feat unmatched in the chart's history.

1977.title: Best of My Love
1977.fun_fact: Produced by Earth, Wind and Fire's Maurice White, the Emotions' gospel-rooted sisters spent five weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 with this exuberant track and won the Grammy for Best R&B Performance by a Duo or Group.

1977.title: Evergreen (Love Theme from A Star Is Born)
1977.fun_fact: Barbra Streisand co-wrote this Academy Award-winning song with Paul Williams after receiving a melody she had composed on a cassette tape; it spent three weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 and won the Oscar for Best Original Song.

1977.title: Angel in Your Arms
1977.fun_fact: This Alabama trio's only major hit reached #6 on the Hot 100 with its Southern soul sound and stands as a perfectly crafted example of the kind of radio-friendly soul-pop that defined AM radio in the late 1970s.

1977.title: I Like Dreamin'
1977.fun_fact: Kenny Nolan had previously written 'My Eyes Adored You' for Frankie Valli and 'Lady Marmalade' for Labelle before achieving success as a performer himself with this soft rock ballad that reached #3 on the Hot 100.

1977.title: Don't Leave Me This Way
1977.fun_fact: A cover of Harold Melvin and the Blue Notes' 1975 recording, Thelma Houston's more urgent, polished version spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100 and won the Grammy for Best R&B Vocal Performance, Female.

1977.title: (Your Love Has Lifted Me) Higher and Higher
1977.fun_fact: Rita Coolidge's pop version of Jackie Wilson's 1967 soul classic reached #2 on the Hot 100 and won her the Grammy for Best Pop Vocal Performance, Female, in a year when she won two Grammy Awards.

1977.title: Undercover Angel
1977.fun_fact: Alan O'Day, better known as the writer of 'Rock and Roll Heaven' for the Righteous Brothers, spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100 with this dream-sequence pop fantasy as a performer in his only major chart success.

1977.title: Torn Between Two Lovers
1977.fun_fact: Written by Peter Yarrow of Peter, Paul and Mary and Phil Jarrell, this confession of loving two people simultaneously spent two weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 and generated significant controversy on radio despite its gentle melodic approach.

1977.title: I'm Your Boogie Man
1977.fun_fact: KC and the Sunshine Band's continued commercial dominance in 1977 produced this #1 hit, spending one week at the top and demonstrating that their irresistible Miami funk formula had remarkable staying power on the pop charts.

1977.title: Dancing Queen
1977.fun_fact: ABBA's only #1 single on the Hot 100 was reportedly inspired after the band attended a party where they heard people dancing with particular joy; it spent one week at the top and remains one of the most played pop songs in history.

1977.title: You Make Me Feel Like Dancing
1977.fun_fact: Leo Sayer's disco-influenced track spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100 and won the Grammy for Best Rhythm and Blues Song, a surprising category win for a British pop singer not primarily associated with the genre.

1977.title: Margaritaville
1977.fun_fact: Jimmy Buffett wrote this about his own hedonistic life of drinking and irresponsibility in Key West; it reached #8 on the Hot 100 and spawned an entire lifestyle brand, restaurant chain, and cultural movement known as Parrothead culture.

1977.title: Telephone Line
1977.fun_fact: Jeff Lynne wrote this about being unable to connect emotionally in a phone conversation and it reached #7 on the Hot 100, combining the orchestral rock grandeur that defined ELO with a surprisingly intimate and vulnerable lyrical perspective.

1977.title: Whatcha Gonna Do?
1977.fun_fact: This San Francisco soft rock group's debut hit reached #6 on the Hot 100 and was produced with the polished, sun-drenched sound of California yacht rock that was becoming increasingly dominant on adult contemporary radio.

1977.title: Do You Wanna Make Love
1977.fun_fact: Peter McCann, a Nashville songwriter, scored a one-hit wonder with this direct and cheerfully unambiguous pop proposition that reached #5 on the Hot 100 before retreating to songwriting for other artists.

1977.title: Sir Duke
1977.fun_fact: Written as a tribute to Duke Ellington following his death in 1974, Stevie Wonder also namechecked Count Basie, Glenn Miller, Louis Armstrong, and Ella Fitzgerald, and the track spent three weeks at #1 on the Hot 100.

1977.title: Hotel California
1977.fun_fact: Don Felder created the guitar chord progression on a demo at his Malibu beach house; Don Henley's lyrics depicted the seductive corruption of the California dream, and the track spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100.

1977.title: Got to Give It Up
1977.fun_fact: Marvin Gaye recorded this at a party he hosted at the recording studio, capturing the ambient sounds of guests talking and laughing; it spent two weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 and inspired a decades-long copyright case involving Robin Thicke's 'Blurred Lines.'

1977.title: Gonna Fly Now
1977.fun_fact: The theme from Rocky spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100 and has become one of the most recognizable pieces of music in film history, synonymous with athletic motivation and personal triumph worldwide.

1977.title: Southern Nights
1977.fun_fact: Glen Campbell's cover of Allen Toussaint's atmospheric New Orleans song spent two weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 and many listeners were surprised to discover it was not original material given how perfectly Campbell made it his own.

1977.title: Rich Girl
1977.fun_fact: Daryl Hall wrote 'Rich Girl' inspired by a real acquaintance but changed the gender to make it about a woman; it spent two weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 and was Hall and Oates's first chart-topper as a duo.

1977.title: When I Need You
1977.fun_fact: Written by Carole Bayer Sager and Albert Hammond as a musical equivalent of a love letter, Leo Sayer's version spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100 and reached #1 simultaneously in several European countries.

1977.title: Hot Line
1977.fun_fact: Produced by Freddie Perren, the Sylvers' follow-up to 'Boogie Fever' reached #5 on the Hot 100 and continued their run as one of Perren's most commercially successful projects during his extraordinary mid-1970s productivity.

1977.title: Car Wash
1977.fun_fact: Written and produced by Norman Whitfield for the film of the same name, this disco-soul track spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100 and was part of one of the most successful blaxploitation film soundtracks not associated with Isaac Hayes.

1977.title: You Don't Have to Be a Star (To Be in My Show)
1977.fun_fact: Former 5th Dimension members Marilyn McCoo and Billy Davis Jr., who were married in real life, won the Grammy for Best Rhythm and Blues Performance by a Duo or Group for this track which spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100.

1977.title: Fly Like an Eagle
1977.fun_fact: Steve Miller wrote this in 1973 but waited three years to record it; its dreamy synthesizer opening became one of classic rock's most recognizable sounds and the track reached #2 on the Hot 100.

1977.title: Don't Give Up on Us
1977.fun_fact: British-American actor David Soul, known for playing Hutch on Starsky and Hutch, spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100 with this easy listening ballad, one of the more unlikely pop chart victories by a television star.

1977.title: On and On
1977.fun_fact: Stephen Bishop wrote this wistful acoustic track while working as a session guitarist and it reached #11 on the Hot 100, launching his career as a singer-songwriter and leading to songwriting collaborations with Art Garfunkel and others.

1977.title: Feels Like the First Time
1977.fun_fact: Written by Mick Jones as Foreigner's debut single, this reached #4 on the Hot 100 and introduced the transatlantic rock group to American audiences as a major commercial force in the emerging arena rock genre.

1977.title: Couldn't Get It Right
1977.fun_fact: The British Climax Blues Band's funk-rock track reached #3 on the Hot 100 in 1977 and was a surprise commercial hit for a group better known for their work in the British blues scene than mainstream American pop.

1977.title: Easy
1977.fun_fact: Lionel Richie wrote this breakup song in about 45 minutes and it reached #4 on the Hot 100, featuring one of the decade's most emotionally direct lead vocals and foreshadowing the ballad-focused solo career Richie would pursue in the 1980s.

1977.title: Right Time of the Night
1977.fun_fact: Written by Peter McCann, Jennifer Warnes's intimate recording reached #6 on the Hot 100 and launched her career as a solo artist separate from her work as a backing vocalist for Leonard Cohen and others.

1977.title: I've Got Love on My Mind
1977.fun_fact: Chuck Jackson and Marvin Yancy wrote this for Natalie Cole and it spent two weeks at #1 on the R&B chart while reaching #5 on the Hot 100 during her remarkable run of hits produced by Yancy, who later became her husband.

1977.title: Blinded by the Light
1977.fun_fact: Originally written by Bruce Springsteen and recorded on his 1973 debut album, Manfred Mann's rearranged version spent two weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 and gave Springsteen his first #1 hit as a songwriter.

1977.title: Looks Like We Made It
1977.fun_fact: Richard Kerr and Will Jennings wrote this farewell-to-a-relationship ballad and Barry Manilow's recording spent two weeks at #1 on the Hot 100, continuing his extraordinary run as the most commercially dominant adult contemporary artist of the era.

1977.title: So in to You
1977.fun_fact: The Atlanta Rhythm Section's laid-back Southern rock track reached #7 on the Hot 100 and introduced this Georgia studio band to national audiences, setting the stage for their even larger commercial moment with 'Imaginary Lover' the following year.

1977.title: Dreams
1977.fun_fact: Stevie Nicks wrote this in about ten minutes in Sly Stone's recording studio while taking a break from the Rumours sessions; it spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100 and remains Fleetwood Mac's only chart-topping single in the United States.

1977.title: Enjoy Yourself
1977.fun_fact: The Jacksons' first major hit after leaving Motown for Epic Records reached #6 on the Hot 100 and marked the beginning of a new creative chapter that would eventually culminate in Michael Jackson's solo breakthrough.

1977.title: Dazz
1977.fun_fact: Atlanta funk group Brick coined the term 'dazz' to mean a combination of disco and jazz, and their debut single reached #3 on the Hot 100 in a genre-blending moment that anticipated the jazz-funk fusion of the 1980s.

1977.title: I'm in You
1977.fun_fact: The follow-up to the massive success of Frampton Comes Alive, this title track from Frampton's next studio album reached #2 on the Hot 100 but the album ultimately disappointed commercially, beginning a career downturn.

1977.title: Lucille
1977.fun_fact: Written by Roger Bowling and Hal Bynum, Kenny Rogers's delivery of this tale of a woman abandoning her family won him the Grammy for Best Male Country Vocal Performance and reached #5 on the Hot 100 in his commercial breakthrough moment.

1977.title: The Things We Do for Love
1977.fun_fact: 10cc's co-founders Kevin Godley and Lol Creme had just left the group when Eric Stewart and Graham Gouldman wrote and recorded this without them; it reached #5 on the Hot 100 and was a commercial reaffirmation of the remaining duo's abilities.

1977.title: Da Doo Ron Ron
1977.fun_fact: The former Hardy Boy's bubblegum cover of the Crystals' 1963 Phil Spector production spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100 and generated teen idol hysteria comparable to the original British Invasion at Shaun Cassidy's live performances.

1977.title: Handy Man
1977.fun_fact: James Taylor's smooth pop cover of Jimmy Jones's 1959 recording spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100 and won him the Grammy for Best Male Pop Vocal Performance, his second Grammy for a cover version after 'You've Got a Friend.'

1977.title: Just a Song Before I Go
1977.fun_fact: Graham Nash wrote this in 15 minutes after accepting a bet that he couldn't write a hit song that quickly; it reached #7 on the Hot 100 and was Crosby, Stills and Nash's biggest chart success in years.

1977.title: You and Me
1977.fun_fact: Alice Cooper's most commercially successful ballad spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100 and was written with Dick Wagner as a deliberate attempt to demonstrate that the shock rock showman could deliver genuine emotional vulnerability.

1977.title: Swayin' to the Music (Slow Dancing)
1977.fun_fact: Johnny Rivers's final major pop hit reached #10 on the Hot 100 and was a warm, nostalgic slow dance ballad that captured the mood of the late-disco era when couples were looking for an alternative to the frenzied floor.

1977.title: Lonely Boy
1977.fun_fact: Andrew Gold wrote this melancholy character study about a boy displaced by a new sibling and it reached #7 on the Hot 100 while becoming one of the most enduring soft rock recordings of the late 1970s.

1977.title: I Wish
1977.fun_fact: Stevie Wonder wrote this nostalgic recollection of childhood mischief during the recording sessions for Songs in the Key of Life, and it spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100, propelled by one of the decade's most infectious bass lines.

1977.title: Don't Stop
1977.fun_fact: Christine McVie wrote this optimistic breakup song about her ex-husband John McVie, deliberately choosing an uplifting perspective on ending a relationship; it reached #3 on the Hot 100 and later became Bill Clinton's 1992 presidential campaign theme.

1977.title: Barracuda
1977.fun_fact: Ann Wilson wrote this after a record label executive spread false rumors about her relationship with her sister Nancy to generate publicity; it reached #11 on the Hot 100 and remains Heart's most enduring and fiercest recording.

1977.title: Strawberry Letter 23
1977.fun_fact: Written by Shuggie Otis in 1971 and recorded with Quincy Jones producing, the Brothers Johnson's version of this psychedelic funk track reached #5 on the Hot 100 and remains one of the most distinctive productions of the era.

1977.title: Night Moves
1977.artist: Bob Seger & The Silver Bullet Band
1977.fun_fact: Bob Seger wrote this nostalgic memoir of teenage romance in the back seat of a car during a Michigan summer, and it reached #4 on the Hot 100, establishing him as one of rock's great working-class storytellers.

1977.title: You're My World
1977.fun_fact: Helen Reddy's cover of the Italian ballad 'Il Mio Mondo,' previously recorded by Cilla Black in English, reached #18 on the Hot 100 as part of her continuing run of adult contemporary chart success through the late 1970s.

1977.title: Heard It in a Love Song
1977.fun_fact: Written by Toy Caldwell about the transient romantic relationships of life on the road as a touring musician, this reached #14 on the Hot 100 and was the Marshall Tucker Band's biggest pop crossover moment.

1977.title: Carry On Wayward Son
1977.fun_fact: Kerry Livgren wrote this progressive rock anthem the night before the band was scheduled to record their Leftoverture album; it reached #11 on the Hot 100 and has become the definitive statement of the heartland progressive rock sound.

1977.title: New Kid in Town
1977.fun_fact: Don Henley, Glenn Frey, and J.D. Souther wrote this meditation on fame's transience and the ease with which audiences replace their idols; it spent two weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 from the Hotel California album.

1977.title: My Heart Belongs to Me
1977.fun_fact: Alan Gordon wrote this declaration of personal autonomy specifically for Barbra Streisand and it reached #4 on the Hot 100 as part of her sustained commercial run through the late 1970s.

1977.title: After the Lovin'
1977.fun_fact: Written by Alan Bernstein and Ritchie Adams, Engelbert Humperdinck's tender recording spent one week at #1 on the Adult Contemporary chart and reached #8 on the Hot 100, giving him his biggest American hit in nearly a decade.

1977.title: Jet Airliner
1977.fun_fact: Written by Paul Pena, who later became famous for learning Tuvan throat singing and competing in a Mongolian competition, Steve Miller's rock version reached #8 on the Hot 100 and became one of his most recognizable FM radio staples.

1977.title: Stand Tall
1977.fun_fact: Former Guess Who frontman Burton Cummings's first major solo hit reached #10 on the Hot 100 and was a confident statement of artistic independence following years of being associated primarily with his former band.

1977.title: Way Down
1977.fun_fact: One of the last recordings Elvis Presley completed before his death on August 16, 1977, this country-influenced track reached #18 on the Hot 100 and topped the country chart, with his death dramatically accelerating its chart ascent.

1977.title: Weekend in New England
1977.fun_fact: Written by Randy Edelman about a long-distance romance, Barry Manilow's recording reached #10 on the Hot 100 and was one of his most emotionally sophisticated ballads, appreciated even by critics who dismissed his more overtly commercial work.

1977.title: It Was Almost Like a Song
1977.fun_fact: Written by Hal David and Archie Jordan, Ronnie Milsap's country-pop crossover reached #16 on the Hot 100 and was one of several hits the blind Alabama singer achieved through appealing to both country and adult contemporary audiences.

1977.title: Smoke from a Distant Fire
1977.fun_fact: This Alabama duo's only major hit reached #9 on the Hot 100 with a sound that blended Southern rock with the blue-eyed soul influences that were becoming commercially dominant in the late 1970s.

1977.title: Cold as Ice
1977.fun_fact: Mick Jones and Lou Gramm wrote this character study of an emotionally withholding partner and it reached #6 on the Hot 100, becoming one of Foreigner's most enduring classic rock radio staples alongside their other early hits.

1977.title: Ariel
1977.fun_fact: Dean Friedman's charming, conversational pop song about meeting a girl at a farmer's market reached #26 on the Hot 100 but performed far better in the UK, reaching #1 and making him briefly one of Britain's most unexpected pop sensations.

1977.title: Lost Without Your Love
1977.fun_fact: David Gates reunited Bread for one final album and this reached #9 on the Hot 100, demonstrating that the group's melodic soft rock formula retained genuine commercial viability even after a five-year hiatus.

1977.title: Star Wars Theme/Cantina Band
1977.fun_fact: Producers Harold Wheeler and Meco Monardo created this disco arrangement of John Williams's Star Wars score, spending two weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 and becoming one of the fastest-selling singles in 20th Century Records' history.

1977.title: Float On
1977.fun_fact: Each member of this Detroit vocal group introduces himself by his astrological sign before singing to a woman born under that sign; the result reached #2 on the Hot 100 and was one of the most charmingly unusual conceptual approaches to a pop single.

1977.title: Jeans On
1977.fun_fact: Originally written as a television commercial for Brutus jeans, Lord David Dundas (an actual British aristocrat) transformed the jingle into a pop single that reached #17 on the Hot 100 in one of the era's more successful advertising crossovers.

1977.title: Lido Shuffle
1977.fun_fact: Boz Scaggs wrote this about a fictional gambler named Lido and it reached #11 on the Hot 100 during his commercial peak, with studio musicians David Paich and David Foster who worked on his Silk Degrees album later forming Toto.

1977.title: Keep It Comin' Love
1977.fun_fact: Another hit from KC and the Sunshine Band's extraordinary commercial run, this reached #2 on the Hot 100 and was part of their remarkable achievement of placing multiple #1 hits in a single calendar year.

1977.title: You Made Me Believe in Magic
1977.fun_fact: The Bay City Rollers' final major US hit reached #10 on the Hot 100 as the tartan-clad Scottish teen idols' moment of American popularity began to wane with the rise of punk and new wave.

1977.title: Livin' Thing
1977.fun_fact: Jeff Lynne wrote this after the inspiration struck him just before a recording session; its distinctive cello hook and complex multi-tracked production reached #13 on the Hot 100 and showcased ELO's orchestral rock ambitions at their most realized.

1977.title: Give a Little Bit
1977.fun_fact: Written by Roger Hodgson in his late teens and shelved for years before being recorded, this acoustic-flavored pop track reached #15 on the Hot 100 and was Supertramp's commercial breakthrough in the United States.

1977.title: That's Rock 'n' Roll
1977.fun_fact: Shaun Cassidy's second major hit of 1977 reached #3 on the Hot 100 and continued his brief but commercially significant moment as a teen pop idol following in the footsteps of his older half-brother David Cassidy.

1977.title: Love So Right
1977.fun_fact: Written by Barry, Robin, and Maurice Gibb during their transitional period between ballad-focused pop and disco dominance, this reached #3 on the Hot 100 and was part of the Children of the World album that bridged their two commercial eras.

1977.title: The Rubberband Man
1977.fun_fact: Thom Bell and Linda Creed wrote this uplifting track about a street musician's unifying effect on a neighborhood; it reached #2 on the Hot 100 and the infectious elastic bass line became one of Philadelphia soul's most enduring and immediately recognizable sounds.

1977.title: I Never Cry
1977.fun_fact: Alice Cooper's second entry in the 1977 year-end Hot 100 reached #12 on the Hot 100 and demonstrated his range from theatrical shock rock to confessional ballads, a duality that distinguished him from one-dimensional shock acts of the era.

1977.title: Nobody Does It Better
1977.fun_fact: Written by Marvin Hamlisch and Carole Bayer Sager for the James Bond film The Spy Who Loved Me, Carly Simon's sultry recording reached #2 on the Hot 100 and earned Grammy and Academy Award nominations.

1977.title: High School Dance
1977.fun_fact: The Sylvers' third entry in 1977 chart activity reached #17 on the Hot 100 and was one of several Freddie Perren productions that kept the family group commercially active through this period.

1977.title: Love's Grown Deep
1977.fun_fact: Kenny Nolan's second solo hit reached #20 on the Hot 100 and followed up the success of 'I Like Dreamin' with a similarly warm, radio-friendly soft pop ballad that fit perfectly into the late-1970s adult contemporary format.

1977.title: Ain't Gonna Bump No More (With No Big Fat Woman)
1977.fun_fact: Joe Tex's final major pop hit was this comedic disco novelty that reached #12 on the Hot 100 and captured the bump dance craze with a good-natured humor that made it one of the more cheerfully absurd pop hits of the period.

1977.title: I Wanna Get Next to You
1977.fun_fact: The second Rose Royce song in the 1977 year-end chart, this reached #10 on the Hot 100 from the Car Wash soundtrack and was a more straightforward soul ballad that showcased the group's versatility within the Norman Whitfield production style.

1977.title: Somebody to Love
1977.fun_fact: Freddie Mercury wrote this as his gospel-rock homage to Aretha Franklin after being inspired by her vocal style, and it reached #13 on the Hot 100 with a massive multi-tracked vocal choir sound that Mercury created by overdubbing himself repeatedly.

1977.title: Muskrat Love
1977.fun_fact: A cover of America's 1973 recording, Captain and Tennille's version reached #4 on the Hot 100 and was performed at the White House for Queen Elizabeth II during her bicentennial visit, reportedly to Her Majesty's considerable bemusement.

1977.title: Walk This Way
1977.fun_fact: Steven Tyler and Joe Perry wrote this after being inspired by a riff Perry played; it reached #10 on the Hot 100 and later became globally iconic in 1986 when Run-DMC collaborated with Aerosmith on a hip-hop remake that broke multiple genre barriers.

1977.title: Cherchez La Femme
1977.fun_fact: This New York disco group blended swing era big band sounds with 1970s funk and disco in a uniquely theatrical style, and their debut single reached #27 on the Hot 100 while becoming a major club hit well beyond its chart position.

1977.title: Year of the Cat
1977.fun_fact: Al Stewart wrote this about an actress he met and the mysterious, cat-like quality she possessed; it reached #8 on the Hot 100 and remains one of the most distinctive and atmospheric recordings of the late-1970s soft rock era.

1977.title: Boogie Nights
1977.fun_fact: Written by Rod Temperton, who later wrote 'Thriller' for Michael Jackson, Heatwave's debut single reached #2 on the Hot 100 and introduced one of the decade's most gifted pop songwriters to international audiences.

1977.title: Go Your Own Way
1977.fun_fact: Lindsey Buckingham wrote this about his deteriorating relationship with Stevie Nicks; she objected to the lyric suggesting she wanted to be with other men, but Buckingham refused to change it, and it reached #10 on the Hot 100.

1977.title: Sorry Seems to Be the Hardest Word
1977.fun_fact: Written by Elton John and Bernie Taupin during a period of personal and professional difficulty for both men, this melancholy ballad reached #6 on the Hot 100 and was one of the most emotionally unguarded recordings of Elton John's career.

1977.title: Don't Worry Baby
1977.fun_fact: B.J. Thomas's cover of the Beach Boys' 1964 classic reached #17 on the Hot 100 and was part of his ongoing strategy of revisiting earlier pop material as new original hits became harder to achieve for established artists.

1977.title: Knowing Me, Knowing You
1977.fun_fact: ABBA wrote this song about divorce at a time when both couples in the group were experiencing serious marital difficulties; it reached #14 on the Hot 100 and was one of the first major pop songs to address the emotional specifics of a marriage ending.

1977.title: How Much Love
1977.fun_fact: Leo Sayer's third entry in the 1977 year-end Hot 100 reached #17 on the Hot 100 and was part of his extraordinarily productive year, during which he placed more songs on the chart than almost any other British artist.

1977.title: Star Wars (Main Title)
1977.fun_fact: John Williams conducted the London Symphony Orchestra for the Star Wars soundtrack, and this classical recording of the main theme reached #10 on the Hot 100, one of the rare instances of a full orchestra single achieving mainstream pop chart success.

1977.title: Devil's Gun
1977.fun_fact: This Detroit funk group's disco track reached #36 on the Hot 100 but performed strongly enough in extended club play versions to make the year-end ranking, demonstrating the gap between radio singles charts and actual cultural impact in the disco era.

1978.title: Shadow Dancing
1978.fun_fact: Written by all four Gibb brothers, Andy Gibb's third consecutive #1 single spent seven weeks at the top of the Hot 100 and made him the only solo artist in chart history to have his first three singles all reach #1.

1978.title: Night Fever
1978.fun_fact: The Bee Gees wrote this for Saturday Night Fever after director John Badham told them he needed a song that captured the feeling of being in a packed disco; it spent eight weeks at #1 on the Hot 100, the longest run of any 1978 single.

1978.title: You Light Up My Life
1978.fun_fact: Joe Brooks wrote this song and it spent ten weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 in 1977, making it one of the longest-running chart-toppers in pop history; Debby Boone's devout Christian interpretation gave it a spiritual dimension its composer had not originally intended.

1978.title: Stayin' Alive
1978.fun_fact: Written after Barry Gibb sang the words 'staying alive' while the brothers were brainstorming, this spent four weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 and its BPM of 103.3 has made it the song most commonly recommended by medical professionals for maintaining CPR chest compression rhythm.

1978.title: Kiss You All Over
1978.fun_fact: British songwriting team Mike Chapman and Nicky Chinn wrote this for the Kentucky country band Exile, and it spent four weeks at #1 on the Hot 100, a remarkable commercial crossover for a group that had been a struggling club act just months earlier.

1978.title: How Deep Is Your Love
1978.fun_fact: The Bee Gees wrote this after their Saturday Night Fever collaborator Yvonne Elliman mentioned she wanted a ballad; it spent three weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 and was the first of the Saturday Night Fever singles to reach the top of the chart.

1978.title: Baby Come Back
1978.fun_fact: Written by Peter Beckett and J.C. Crowley during a late-night session fueled by the emotional pain of a recent breakup, this spent three weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 and was the only major hit for the Los Angeles group Player.

1978.title: (Love Is) Thicker Than Water
1978.fun_fact: Co-written by Andy Gibb and Barry Gibb, this spent two weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 and was the third Gibb-related recording to reach the top of the chart within a few months, a period of almost unparalleled chart dominance for one family.

1978.title: Boogie Oogie Oogie
1978.fun_fact: This Los Angeles group's debut single spent three weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 and won the Grammy for Best New Artist, though the group struggled commercially with subsequent releases despite their auspicious beginning.

1978.title: Three Times a Lady
1978.fun_fact: Lionel Richie wrote this as a tribute to his parents at their 37th wedding anniversary party after his father's toast moved him deeply; it spent two weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 and gave the Commodores their first ballad-only #1.

1978.title: Grease
1978.fun_fact: Barry Gibb wrote this title song for the Grease film in a single afternoon, deliberately evoking 1950s doo-wop style, and Frankie Valli's recording spent two weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 as the film became one of the highest-grossing musicals in cinema history.

1978.title: I Go Crazy
1978.fun_fact: Paul Davis spent two years writing and recording this before finally releasing it; the song spent 40 weeks on the Hot 100 and reached #7 in one of the longest chart runs of the era, demonstrating that slow-building word-of-mouth could still work in the radio age.

1978.title: You're the One That I Want
1978.fun_fact: Written by John Farrar in a single afternoon specifically for the Grease film, this spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100 and became one of the best-selling singles of all time globally, selling over fifteen million copies worldwide.

1978.title: Emotion
1978.fun_fact: Written and produced by Barry and Robin Gibb, who also provided backing vocals, this reached #3 on the Hot 100 and was the biggest hit for Australian singer Samantha Sang, who was a close friend of the Gibb family.

1978.title: Lay Down Sally
1978.fun_fact: Eric Clapton wrote this reggae-influenced track after being inspired by a Bob Marley concert in Jamaica; it reached #3 on the Hot 100 and was a deliberately relaxed and unhurried recording that contrasted with the urgency of his earlier work.

1978.title: Miss You
1978.fun_fact: The Rolling Stones' first full disco-influenced recording spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100; Mick Jagger reportedly wrote the lyric 'I been walking Central Park' after actually spending many late nights there while recording in New York.

1978.title: Just the Way You Are
1978.fun_fact: Billy Joel wrote this for his wife Elizabeth Weber at the request of his friend Richie Havens's manager, as a wedding gift; Elizabeth later divorced him and retained rights to the song, which reached #3 on the Hot 100 and won Grammy Awards for Record of the Year and Song of the Year.

1978.title: With a Little Luck
1978.fun_fact: Paul McCartney's optimistic track spent two weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 and was one of Wings' final major American hits before the group effectively dissolved following the murder of John Lennon and McCartney's own cannabis arrest in Japan.

1978.title: If I Can't Have You
1978.fun_fact: Written by the Bee Gees specifically for the Saturday Night Fever soundtrack, Yvonne Elliman's recording spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100 and was her commercial breakthrough after years of performing backup vocals and opening for Eric Clapton.

1978.title: Dance, Dance, Dance (Yowsah, Yowsah, Yowsah)
1978.fun_fact: Nile Rodgers and Bernard Edwards were initially turned away from a Studio 54 party and wrote this in anger about the experience; the track reached #6 on the Hot 100 and launched one of the most influential production partnerships in pop history.

1978.title: Feels So Good
1978.fun_fact: Flugelhorn player Chuck Mangione spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100 with this jazz fusion instrumental and won the Grammy for Best Pop Instrumental Performance, one of the most commercially successful jazz crossover recordings of the decade.

1978.title: Hot Child in the City
1978.fun_fact: Written by Nick Gilder and James McCulloch about runaway teenagers on Hollywood Boulevard, this reached #1 on the Hot 100 and was Gilder's only major US hit despite his considerable success in Canada.

1978.title: Love Is Like Oxygen
1978.fun_fact: Andy Scott and Trevor Griffin wrote this ambitious art-pop track that reached #8 on the Hot 100 and represented Sweet's final significant commercial moment as they attempted to evolve beyond their glam rock origins.

1978.title: It's a Heartache
1978.fun_fact: Welsh singer Bonnie Tyler recorded this in one take despite suffering from vocal nodules that gave her voice its distinctive raspy quality; it reached #3 on the Hot 100 and the unusual texture of her voice became her most commercially valuable asset.

1978.title: We Will Rock You / We Are the Champions
1978.fun_fact: Freddie Mercury wrote 'We Are the Champions' after a particularly triumphant concert, and Brian May wrote 'We Will Rock You' as a song the audience could participate in; released as a double A-side, they reached #4 on the Hot 100 and became two of sport's most universal anthems.

1978.title: Baker Street
1978.fun_fact: Gerry Rafferty wrote this about his feelings of alienation and confusion during his lengthy legal battle to leave Stealers Wheel; the saxophone riff, played by Raphael Ravenscroft, became one of the most recognizable in pop music history and the track reached #2 on the Hot 100.

1978.title: Can't Smile Without You
1978.fun_fact: Written by Chris Arnold, David Martin, and Geoff Morrow, Barry Manilow's recording spent one week at #3 on the Hot 100 and continued his extraordinary run as the most consistently charting adult contemporary artist of the late 1970s.

1978.title: Too Much, Too Little, Too Late
1978.fun_fact: This beautifully matched pairing of Deniece Williams's soprano with Johnny Mathis's warm tenor spent two weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 and won the Grammy for Best Pop Performance by a Duo or Group.

1978.title: Dance With Me
1978.fun_fact: Peter Brown's sophisticated dance track reached #8 on the Hot 100 and was produced with a jazz-inflected disco sophistication that distinguished it from the more formulaic recordings flooding the market as disco's commercial peak approached.

1978.title: Two Out of Three Ain't Bad
1978.fun_fact: Jim Steinman wrote this as part of the Bat Out of Hell rock opera, and Meat Loaf's bombastic delivery reached #11 on the Hot 100; the parent album eventually sold over 40 million copies worldwide, one of the best-selling albums in history.

1978.title: Jack and Jill
1978.fun_fact: Ray Parker Jr.'s debut single with his band Raydio reached #8 on the Hot 100 and launched his career as both a recording artist and prolific songwriter; he later wrote the Ghostbusters theme which became one of the 1980s' most recognized movie songs.

1978.title: Take a Chance on Me
1978.fun_fact: Bjorn Andersson wrote the central riff by humming it to himself while jogging; ABBA's recording spent three weeks at #1 on the UK chart and reached #3 on the Hot 100, reinforcing their status as the world's most commercially successful pop group.

1978.title: Sometimes When We Touch
1978.fun_fact: Dan Hill co-wrote this nakedly emotional ballad about the vulnerability of intimacy with Barry Mann; it reached #3 on the Hot 100 and became the Canadian singer's signature recording, though he struggled to follow its enormous commercial success.

1978.title: Last Dance
1978.fun_fact: Written by Paul Jabara for the film Thank God It's Friday, Donna Summer's recording won the Academy Award for Best Original Song and the Grammy for Best R&B Vocal Performance, Female, reaching #3 on the Hot 100.

1978.title: Hopelessly Devoted to You
1978.fun_fact: John Farrar wrote this for the Grease film after the studio decided more songs were needed during post-production; Olivia Newton-John's recording reached #3 on the Hot 100 and earned an Academy Award nomination for Best Original Song.

1978.title: Hot Blooded
1978.fun_fact: Mick Jones wrote this straightforward rock anthem about sexual attraction in about 20 minutes, intentionally keeping the sentiment simple and direct; it reached #3 on the Hot 100 and became one of Foreigner's most enduring classic rock radio staples.

1978.title: You're in My Heart (The Final Acclaim)
1978.fun_fact: Rod Stewart wrote this as a tribute to his relationship with the Scots football club Celtic and his then-girlfriend Britt Ekland, combining both loves into one track; it reached #4 on the Hot 100.

1978.title: The Closer I Get to You
1978.fun_fact: The second major collaboration between Roberta Flack and Donny Hathaway reached #2 on the Hot 100 and was released less than a year before Donny Hathaway's tragic death in January 1979.

1978.title: Dust in the Wind
1978.fun_fact: Kerry Livgren wrote this after reading a Native American poem about the ephemeral nature of existence; it reached #6 on the Hot 100 and was Kansas's most unexpected hit, an acoustic folk ballad from a band known for complex progressive rock.

1978.title: Magnet and Steel
1978.fun_fact: Stevie Nicks and Lindsey Buckingham provided background vocals on this track, inspired by Egan's feelings for Nicks herself; it reached #8 on the Hot 100 and was the only major US hit for this California singer-songwriter.

1978.title: Short People
1978.fun_fact: Randy Newman wrote this as obvious satire of prejudice and bigotry, but many listeners took it literally and the Little People of America protested it; despite or because of the controversy, it spent one week at #2 on the Hot 100.

1978.title: Use ta Be My Girl
1978.fun_fact: Gamble and Huff produced this funky farewell to an ex-girlfriend and it reached #4 on the Hot 100, continuing the O'Jays' run as Philadelphia International's most commercially consistent act throughout the label's golden decade.

1978.title: Our Love
1978.fun_fact: Written by David Foster, Alan Rich, and Jud Friedman, this reached #10 on the Hot 100 and was part of Natalie Cole's productive late-1970s period before health struggles temporarily derailed her career.

1978.title: Love Will Find a Way
1978.fun_fact: Pablo Cruise's biggest US hit reached #6 on the Hot 100 and was one of the quintessential yacht rock recordings, featuring the polished, sun-drenched California production sound that defined the adult contemporary genre in the late 1970s.

1978.title: An Everlasting Love
1978.fun_fact: Barry Gibb wrote this for his younger brother Andy, and it reached #5 on the Hot 100 during the Gibb family's period of extraordinary chart dominance, with Andy becoming the first solo artist to have three consecutive #1 singles.

1978.title: Love Is in the Air
1978.fun_fact: Written by Harry Vanda and George Young (older brothers of AC/DC's Angus and Malcolm Young), this Australian pop track reached #7 on the Hot 100 and became one of the decade's most enduring radio staples outside its home country.

1978.title: Goodbye Girl
1978.fun_fact: Written by Neil Diamond's frequent collaborator for the 1977 Richard Dreyfuss film, David Gates's recording reached #15 on the Hot 100 and demonstrated that he remained a skilled pop craftsman outside his work with Bread.

1978.title: Slip Slidin' Away
1978.fun_fact: Paul Simon wrote this meditation on the elusive nature of one's deepest goals and featured the Oak Ridge Boys on backing vocals in an inspired pairing of New York sophistication and Southern gospel tradition, reaching #5 on the Hot 100.

1978.title: The Groove Line
1978.fun_fact: Rod Temperton wrote this driving funk track for Heatwave and it reached #7 on the Hot 100, further establishing Temperton's reputation as one of the era's most gifted commercial writers before his work with Michael Jackson made him world-famous.

1978.title: Thunder Island
1978.fun_fact: Former Spirit and Jo Jo Gunne member Jay Ferguson wrote this Caribbean-flavored soft rock track that reached #9 on the Hot 100 and was the only significant solo hit of his performing career despite his years of musical experience.

1978.title: Imaginary Lover
1978.fun_fact: Written about the fantasy of a perfect romantic partner who exists only in one's imagination, this reached #7 on the Hot 100 and was the Atlanta Rhythm Section's biggest pop hit, finally translating their regional Southern rock success to national audiences.

1978.title: Still the Same
1978.artist: Bob Seger & The Silver Bullet Band
1978.fun_fact: Bob Seger wrote this about a high-rolling gambler whose cool detachment he both envied and found tragic; it spent one week at #4 on the Hot 100 and was part of the Stranger in Town album that made Seger one of rock's most commercially dominant artists.

1978.title: My Angel Baby
1978.fun_fact: This Texas group's only major hit reached #13 on the Hot 100 with a smooth country-pop production that was perfectly calibrated for the late-1970s adult contemporary radio format.

1978.title: Disco Inferno
1978.fun_fact: Originally released in 1976 with limited success, the Trammps' extended recording found new commercial life after being included in Saturday Night Fever, reaching #11 on the Hot 100 in 1978 and demonstrating the film's extraordinary influence on popular music consumption.

1978.title: On Broadway
1978.fun_fact: George Benson's jazz guitar virtuosity transformed the Drifters' 1963 Leiber and Stoller classic into a showcase for his simultaneous guitar-and-vocal technique, reaching #7 on the Hot 100 and further expanding his crossover pop audience.

1978.title: Come Sail Away
1978.fun_fact: Dennis DeYoung wrote this progressive rock odyssey about escaping earthly limitations, and though the album version exceeds six minutes, the edited single reached #8 on the Hot 100 and became one of Styx's most enduring concert favorites.

1978.title: (Every Time I Turn Around) Back in Love Again
1978.fun_fact: Jeffrey Osborne's exuberant lead vocal on this track reached #4 on the Hot 100 and was one of the final major hits for L.T.D. before Osborne launched his solo career, carrying the same joyful energy to even greater commercial success.

1978.title: This Time I'm in It for Love
1978.fun_fact: Player's follow-up to 'Baby Come Back' reached #10 on the Hot 100 and demonstrated that the group had enough songwriting ability to sustain a career beyond their initial breakthrough, even if they never matched that first hit's commercial peak.

1978.title: You Belong to Me
1978.fun_fact: Carly Simon co-wrote this with Michael McDonald and it reached #6 on the Hot 100; the song reflects Simon's sense of loneliness while her then-husband James Taylor was touring and was one of the most autobiographically direct songs in her catalog.

1978.title: Here You Come Again
1978.fun_fact: Written by Barry Mann and Cynthia Weil, Dolly Parton's pop crossover debut spent one week at #3 on the Hot 100 and crossed her from a solely country audience to mainstream pop listeners, launching the broadest commercial phase of her extraordinary career.

1978.title: Blue Bayou
1978.fun_fact: Linda Ronstadt's lush cover of Roy Orbison's 1961 recording spent three weeks at #3 on the Hot 100 and introduced millions of younger listeners to one of rock and roll's founding vocal stylists.

1978.title: Peg
1978.fun_fact: Steely Dan used so many session guitarists in attempting to get the perfect guitar solo that the track features over ten different attempts; Michael McDonald's backing vocals are also featured, and the track reached #11 on the Hot 100 from the Aja album.

1978.title: You Needed Me
1978.fun_fact: Written by Randy Goodrum about his relationship with God, Anne Murray's secular interpretation of this spiritual dependence spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100 and won the Grammy for Best Female Pop Vocal Performance.

1978.title: Shame
1978.fun_fact: Evelyn King was discovered by a producer while cleaning offices at Sigma Sound Studios in Philadelphia; her debut single reached #9 on the Hot 100 and launched the career of one of disco's most distinctive voices.

1978.title: Reminiscing
1978.fun_fact: Written by Graham Goble about real places in Brisbane, Australia where he had grown up, this Australian group's American breakthrough reached #3 on the Hot 100 and was one of several Little River Band hits that dominated adult contemporary radio in the late 1970s.

1978.title: Count On Me
1978.fun_fact: Mickey Thomas joined Jefferson Starship around this time and his powerful tenor voice gave the group a new commercial direction; this recording reached #8 on the Hot 100 and was one of their final hits before a major lineup overhaul.

1978.title: Baby Hold On
1978.fun_fact: Eddie Money was a police officer in New York before pursuing music; his debut single reached #11 on the Hot 100 and launched a career built on straightforward, working-class rock that resonated with AM radio audiences across America.

1978.title: Hey Deanie
1978.fun_fact: Written by Eric Carmen of the Raspberries, Shaun Cassidy's recording reached #7 on the Hot 100 and was part of his brief but commercially significant run as a teen idol, competing directly with the Leif Garrett and Andy Gibb audiences.

1978.title: Summer Nights
1978.fun_fact: The second Grease duet from John Travolta and Olivia Newton-John reached #5 on the Hot 100 and was written by Jim Jacobs and Warren Casey for the original 1971 stage production, making it one of the few Broadway songs to top pop charts in the disco era.

1978.title: What's Your Name
1978.fun_fact: Released several months before the plane crash that killed Ronnie Van Zant and other band members, this became one of Lynyrd Skynyrd's final recordings, reaching #13 on the Hot 100 in a tragically ironic moment of commercial breakthrough.

1978.title: Don't It Make My Brown Eyes Blue
1978.fun_fact: Richard Leigh wrote this while sitting in a Louisville shopping mall food court; Crystal Gayle's recording won the Grammy for Best Female Country Vocal Performance and reached #2 on the Hot 100 in a complete country-to-pop crossover.

1978.title: Because the Night
1978.fun_fact: Bruce Springsteen gave Patti Smith this unfinished song and she completed the lyrics; it reached #13 on the Hot 100 and remains Patti Smith's biggest commercial hit, a rare intersection of punk credibility and mainstream pop appeal.

1978.title: Every Kinda People
1978.fun_fact: Robert Palmer's Bahamian-influenced track about human diversity reached #16 on the Hot 100 and was one of his early recordings that hinted at the stylistic eclecticism that would make him one of the 1980s' most musically adventurous mainstream artists.

1978.title: Copacabana
1978.fun_fact: Barry Manilow and Jack Feldman co-wrote this tragic nightclub drama as a deliberate tribute to show tune-style storytelling; it reached #8 on the Hot 100 and later became the basis for a television movie and stage musical adaptation.

1978.title: Always and Forever
1978.fun_fact: Written by Rod Temperton, who later wrote 'Thriller' and 'Rock with You' for Michael Jackson, this became Heatwave's most enduring recording, reaching #18 on the Hot 100 and later becoming a wedding reception staple for decades.

1978.title: You and I
1978.fun_fact: Rick James's debut single reached #13 on the Hot 100 and introduced his self-coined 'punk funk' sound to mainstream audiences, beginning his run as one of the most prolific and commercially successful R&B artists of the early 1980s.

1978.title: Serpentine Fire
1978.fun_fact: Maurice White incorporated his interest in ancient Egyptian mythology into the lyrics of this energetic funk track, which reached #13 on the Hot 100 and was part of the All 'N All album that represented one of their most musically ambitious recordings.

1978.title: Sentimental Lady
1978.fun_fact: Bob Welch wrote this during his tenure in Fleetwood Mac and recorded it solo after leaving the group; Christine McVie and Mick Fleetwood appeared on the track and it reached #8 on the Hot 100 in one of rock's more unusual reunion moments.

1978.title: Falling
1978.fun_fact: Nashville duo Lenny LeBlanc and Pete Carr scored their only major hit with this atmospheric ballad that reached #13 on the Hot 100, with Carr's distinctive guitar work providing its most memorable musical element.

1978.title: Don't Let Me Be Misunderstood
1978.fun_fact: A Spanish-influenced disco arrangement of the Animals' 1965 classic, Santa Esmeralda's flamenco-flavored version reached #15 on the Hot 100 and became a staple of European discotheques long after its American chart moment had passed.

1978.title: Bluer Than Blue
1978.fun_fact: Randy Goodrum wrote this about his own experience with depression, and Michael Johnson's gentle acoustic recording reached #12 on the Hot 100 in the summer of 1978, representing one of the more understated pop successes of the year.

1978.title: Running on Empty
1978.fun_fact: Jackson Browne recorded this album of live performances and new recordings entirely on the road, capturing the alienating reality of touring life; the title track reached #11 on the Hot 100 and gave Browne his biggest commercial moment.

1978.title: Whenever I Call You 'Friend'
1978.fun_fact: Kenny Loggins co-wrote this with Melissa Manchester and Stevie Nicks contributed harmonies that elevated it to a genuine pop moment; it reached #5 on the Hot 100 and was one of several successful collaborations that defined the late-1970s California pop sound.

1978.title: Fool (If You Think It's Over)
1978.fun_fact: Sunderland-born Chris Rea's debut American hit reached #12 on the Hot 100 and introduced his warm, gravelly vocal style to US audiences, though his commercial breakthrough in America remained limited compared to his sustained European success.

1978.title: Get Off
1978.fun_fact: Miami group Foxy's disco track reached #9 on the Hot 100 and was one of the final examples of independently distributed Southern disco scoring major mainstream success before the major labels consolidated their control of the genre.

1978.title: Sweet Talkin' Woman
1978.fun_fact: Jeff Lynne wrote this as a deliberate attempt to recreate the wall of sound production style of Phil Spector, layering orchestral strings over a driving rock groove; it reached #17 on the Hot 100 from ELO's Out of the Blue double album.

1978.title: Life's Been Good
1978.fun_fact: Joe Walsh wrote this satirical portrait of rock star excess from personal experience living exactly the lifestyle he was lampooning; the edited single reached #12 on the Hot 100 while the original album version ran over eight minutes.

1978.title: I Love the Nightlife
1978.fun_fact: Alicia Bridges wrote this declaration of disco devotion in about 20 minutes after her songwriting partner Susan Hutcheson suggested the hook; it reached #5 on the Hot 100 and became a gay club anthem that has remained in rotation for over four decades.

1978.title: You Can't Turn Me Off (In the Middle of Turning Me On)
1978.fun_fact: This Motown girl group's upbeat soul track reached #12 on the Hot 100 and was produced by Hal Davis in a style that recalled the classic Motown sound while incorporating contemporary late-1970s disco production values.

1978.title: It's So Easy
1978.fun_fact: Linda Ronstadt's urgent cover of the Crickets' 1958 Buddy Holly-associated recording reached #5 on the Hot 100 and was part of her Living in the USA album that positioned her as one of rock's most versatile and commercially reliable performers.

1978.title: Native New Yorker
1978.fun_fact: Written by Sandy Linzer and Denny Randell, Odyssey's dramatic tale of New York City's effect on a young woman reached #21 on the Hot 100 but performed so strongly in clubs and on R&B radio that it earned a place in the year-end chart.

1978.title: Flash Light
1978.fun_fact: George Clinton built this around a bass synthesizer riff by William 'Bootsy' Collins that became one of the most immediately recognizable bass lines in funk history; it reached #16 on the Hot 100 and remains Parliament's most widely sampled recording.

1978.title: Don't Look Back
1978.fun_fact: Tom Scholz spent two years in his basement studio perfecting Boston's recordings before allowing them to be released; the title track of their second album reached #4 on the Hot 100 and the album sold over seven million copies.

1978.title: Turn to Stone
1978.fun_fact: Jeff Lynne wrote this about being unable to communicate with someone emotionally distant, and ELO's third entry in the 1978 year-end Hot 100 reached #13, confirming their status as one of the year's most commercially productive acts.

1978.title: I Can't Stand the Rain
1978.fun_fact: British group Eruption's cover of Ann Peebles's 1973 soul classic reached #18 on the Hot 100 with a disco-influenced arrangement that stripped away the original's raw emotion in favor of a polished dance floor appeal.

1978.title: Ebony Eyes
1978.fun_fact: Bob Welch's second entry in the 1978 year-end Hot 100 reached #14 on the Hot 100 and was part of his most commercially successful year as a solo artist, finally achieving recognition commensurate with his considerable talent as a guitarist and songwriter.

1978.title: The Name of the Game
1978.fun_fact: ABBA wrote this during their 1977 Australian tour and it reached #12 on the Hot 100, continuing their steady presence on American radio even as their European popularity had reached levels of almost unprecedented commercial dominance.

1978.title: We're All Alone
1978.fun_fact: Written by Boz Scaggs and featured on his Silk Degrees album, Rita Coolidge's more intimate interpretation reached #7 on the Hot 100 and won her a Grammy for Best Pop Vocal Performance, Female, her second Grammy of the year.

1978.title: Hollywood Nights
1978.artist: Bob Seger & The Silver Bullet Band
1978.fun_fact: Bob Seger wrote this about a Midwestern girl overwhelmed by the seductive glamour of Los Angeles; it reached #12 on the Hot 100 and was part of his Stranger in Town album that made him one of 1978's most commercially dominant rock artists.

1978.title: Deacon Blues
1978.fun_fact: Donald Fagen and Walter Becker wrote this character study of a romantic loser who dreams of jazz greatness while accepting mediocrity; it reached #19 on the Hot 100 from the Aja album and has since been cited as one of rock's most lyrically sophisticated recordings.

1979.title: My Sharona
1979.fun_fact: Doug Fieger wrote this about a real girl named Sharona Alperin, his then-girlfriend; the track spent six weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 and was the fastest-selling debut single in Capitol Records' history, with its raw energy seeming to answer the disco era's excesses.

1979.title: Bad Girls
1979.fun_fact: Donna Summer wrote this about the working girls she saw on Sunset Boulevard; it spent five weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 and made her the first artist since The Beatles to have two simultaneous top five albums on the Billboard 200.

1979.title: Le Freak
1979.fun_fact: Nile Rodgers and Bernard Edwards wrote this in a single night after being turned away from Studio 54 on New Year's Eve; it spent six weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 and became the best-selling single in Atlantic Records' history up to that point.

1979.title: Da Ya Think I'm Sexy?
1979.fun_fact: Rod Stewart donated all his royalties from this disco-influenced track to UNICEF after performing it at a United Nations benefit concert; it spent four weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 and divided critics who felt it represented a commercial sellout.

1979.title: Reunited
1979.fun_fact: Freddie Perren produced this reconciliation anthem and it spent four weeks at #1 on the Hot 100, making Herb Fame and Linda Greene (the latest in a series of women to record as 'Peach') one of the most unlikely comeback acts of the decade.

1979.title: I Will Survive
1979.fun_fact: Originally the B-side of 'Substitute,' DJs discovered Gloria Gaynor's declaration of post-breakup resilience and it spent three weeks at #1 on the Hot 100, winning the Grammy for Best Disco Recording and becoming the defining feminist anthem of the decade.

1979.title: Hot Stuff
1979.fun_fact: Giorgio Moroder incorporated rock guitar into this hard disco track at Donna Summer's insistence; it spent three weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 and was the first disco record to win a Grammy for Best Rock Vocal Performance, Female.

1979.title: Y.M.C.A.
1979.fun_fact: Jacques Morali wrote this after visiting a New York YMCA and noticing its diverse clientele; it spent three weeks at #2 on the Hot 100 and the arm-gesture dance it inspired has become one of the most universally recognized participation moments at sporting events worldwide.

1979.title: Ring My Bell
1979.fun_fact: Written by Frederick Knight for an eleven-year-old girl's album and completely repurposed by Anita Ward as an adult disco statement, this spent two weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 in an unlikely chart-topping moment for a Memphis soul singer new to the disco market.

1979.title: Sad Eyes
1979.fun_fact: Written by Robert John himself, this romantic ballad spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100 and was the only major chart-topping hit for the singer who had previously reached the top 10 with 'The Lion Sleeps Tonight' in 1972.

1979.title: Too Much Heaven
1979.fun_fact: The Bee Gees donated all proceeds from this track to the United Nations Children's Fund, ultimately generating over seven million dollars for UNICEF; it spent two weeks at #1 on the Hot 100 and was one of their most musically gentle recordings.

1979.title: MacArthur Park
1979.fun_fact: Donna Summer and Giorgio Moroder transformed Jimmy Webb's 1968 Richard Harris recording into a seventeen-minute disco epic, and the edited single spent three weeks at #1 on the Hot 100, giving Summer her third #1 of 1978-1979.

1979.title: When You're in Love with a Beautiful Woman
1979.fun_fact: Written by Even Stevens, Dr. Hook's recording spent one week at #1 on the UK chart and reached #6 on the Hot 100 in what became one of the group's final major American hits as they evolved from novelty acts to mainstream pop performers.

1979.title: Makin' It
1979.fun_fact: Actor David Naughton recorded this disco track as the theme for a television series and it reached #5 on the Hot 100; Naughton later starred in An American Werewolf in London, making this one of rock's more unusual connections to horror film history.

1979.title: Fire
1979.fun_fact: Bruce Springsteen wrote this but his own recording was not immediately released; the Pointer Sisters' hard-driving version spent two weeks at #2 on the Hot 100 and was one of the few times Springsteen compositions charted higher through other artists' recordings than through his own.

1979.title: Tragedy
1979.fun_fact: Barry Gibb wrote this as a conscious attempt to blend their disco sound with classic country music chord progressions, and it spent two weeks at #1 on the Hot 100, the last of their four Hot 100 #1 singles from the post-Saturday Night Fever period.

1979.title: A Little More Love
1979.fun_fact: Written by John Farrar, Olivia Newton-John's fourth entry in the late 1970s year-end charts reached #3 on the Hot 100 and was her first release following the massive commercial success of Grease, maintaining her position as one of pop's most bankable acts.

1979.title: Heart of Glass
1979.fun_fact: Originally composed as a reggae track Debbie Harry and Chris Stein called 'Once I Had a Love,' the song was transformed into a disco track at producer Mike Chapman's suggestion and spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100.

1979.title: What a Fool Believes
1979.fun_fact: Written by Michael McDonald and Kenny Loggins in one afternoon, this sophisticated blue-eyed soul track spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100 and won Grammy Awards for both Record of the Year and Song of the Year.

1979.title: Good Times
1979.fun_fact: Nile Rodgers and Bernard Edwards wrote this as a defiant celebration during the height of the anti-disco backlash; it spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100 and its bass line was directly sampled to create the Sugarhill Gang's 'Rapper's Delight,' one of hip-hop's foundational recordings.

1979.title: You Don't Bring Me Flowers
1979.fun_fact: A Louisville radio DJ spliced together separate solo recordings by Neil Diamond and Barbra Streisand to create a duet that generated such listener demand that the artists were brought together to record an official version; it spent two weeks at #1 on the Hot 100.

1979.title: Knock on Wood
1979.fun_fact: British singer Amii Stewart's disco update of Eddie Floyd's 1966 Stax recording spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100 and introduced this classic soul song to an entire generation of listeners who had been too young for the original.

1979.title: Stumblin' In
1979.fun_fact: The rock-credentialed Suzi Quatro and Smokie's Chris Norman created an unlikely pop pairing that reached #4 on the Hot 100 with this gentle duet, quite different from either artist's usual harder-edged musical territory.

1979.title: Lead Me On
1979.fun_fact: Maxine Nightingale had a three-year gap between her first major hit 'Right Back Where We Started From' and this follow-up, which reached #5 on the Hot 100 and demonstrated the patience sometimes required for a career beyond a debut hit.

1979.title: Shake Your Body (Down to the Ground)
1979.fun_fact: Written by Michael Jackson and younger brother Randy, this became one of the pre-Thriller-era Michael Jackson recordings that hinted most clearly at the dance floor mastery that would make Thriller the best-selling album in history; it reached #7 on the Hot 100.

1979.title: Don't Cry Out Loud
1979.fun_fact: Written by Peter Allen and Carole Bayer Sager about learning to suppress emotional pain, this reached #10 on the Hot 100 and was Melissa Manchester's commercial breakthrough, combining her theatrical vocal style with an accessible pop ballad construction.

1979.title: The Logical Song
1979.fun_fact: Roger Hodgson wrote this about the disorienting experience of being sent to boarding school at age fourteen and losing his sense of self-identity; it reached #6 on the Hot 100 and was Supertramp's biggest American hit.

1979.title: My Life
1979.fun_fact: Billy Joel wrote this as a declaration of personal independence that he has said was directed at his former wife Elizabeth; it spent one week at #3 on the Hot 100 and later became the theme song for the television series Bosom Buddies, launching Tom Hanks's career.

1979.title: Just When I Needed You Most
1979.fun_fact: Randy VanWarmer wrote this at age sixteen after his father's death and it took years before he recorded it; the finished single spent one week at #4 on the Hot 100 and was his only major commercial success despite its craft and emotional impact.

1979.title: You Can't Change That
1979.fun_fact: Ray Parker Jr.'s follow-up to 'Jack and Jill' reached #9 on the Hot 100 and continued to build his reputation as a hitmaker with a gift for infectious pop hooks that would eventually reach its commercial apex with the Ghostbusters theme.

1979.title: Shake Your Groove Thing
1979.fun_fact: Written by Freddie Perren and Dino Fekaris, this followed the massive success of 'Reunited' and reached #5 on the Hot 100, confirming that Peaches and Herb's commercial comeback was genuine and not a one-single phenomenon.

1979.title: I'll Never Love This Way Again
1979.fun_fact: Written by Richard Kerr and Will Jennings, this became Dionne Warwick's biggest hit in years and reached #5 on the Hot 100, winning her the Grammy for Best Female Pop Vocal Performance and reviving a career that had been commercially dormant for most of the 1970s.

1979.title: Love You Inside Out
1979.fun_fact: The final #1 single the Bee Gees achieved during their late-1970s commercial peak spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100 and marked the effective end of their disco-era dominance before the anti-disco backlash dramatically changed radio formats.

1979.title: I Want You to Want Me
1979.fun_fact: Originally released as a studio track in 1977 without success, this live version from the Budokan arena in Japan reached #7 on the Hot 100 after the Cheap Trick at Budokan album became a massive commercial phenomenon, demonstrating the power of the Japanese market to break American acts.

1979.title: The Main Event/Fight
1979.fun_fact: Recorded for the Barbra Streisand film The Main Event, this disco-flavored track spent one week at #3 on the Hot 100 and demonstrated that Streisand could engage successfully with the contemporary dance music aesthetic without sacrificing her own artistic identity.

1979.title: Mama Can't Buy You Love
1979.fun_fact: Based on a recording by Patrick Juvet and produced by Thom Bell in a deliberate departure from Elton's usual collaborators, this Philadelphia soul-influenced track reached #9 on the Hot 100 and was Elton John's most commercially successful experiment with a different production style.

1979.title: I Was Made for Dancin'
1979.fun_fact: Teen idol Leif Garrett's biggest American hit reached #10 on the Hot 100 and was part of his brief commercial moment as one of the late 1970s' most prominent teen magazine cover subjects, competing for the same audience as Shaun Cassidy and Andy Gibb.

1979.title: After the Love Has Gone
1979.fun_fact: Written by David Foster, Jay Graydon, and Bill Champlin, this sophisticated ballad reached #2 on the Hot 100 and won the Grammy for Best R&B Song, representing Earth Wind and Fire's most successful foray into adult contemporary territory.

1979.title: Heaven Knows
1979.fun_fact: Donna Summer's fifth entry across the 1978-1979 year-end charts demonstrated her extraordinary commercial productivity; this duet with Brooklyn Dreams reached #4 on the Hot 100 and featured Joe 'Bean' Esposito, who later recorded the theme for The Karate Kid.
