1979.title: The Gambler
1979.fun_fact: Written by Don Schlitz, Kenny Rogers spent three weeks at #1 on the country chart and reached #16 on the Hot 100 with this tale of card-playing wisdom, winning the Grammy for Best Male Country Vocal Performance and spawning four television movies.

1979.title: Lotta Love
1979.fun_fact: Written by Neil Young after his relationship with Carole Ann Harris ended, Young gave the song to Larson, his then-girlfriend, and her recording reached #8 on the Hot 100 in a sunny pop arrangement that bore little resemblance to Young's spare demo.

1979.title: Lady
1979.fun_fact: Beeb Birtles and Graham Goble wrote this for Australian group Little River Band, and it reached #10 on the Hot 100 as part of their remarkable run of American adult contemporary hits that belied the relatively modest scale of their Australian homeland fanbase.

1979.title: Heaven Must Have Sent You
1979.fun_fact: Bonnie Pointer's solo cover of the Elgins' 1966 Motown recording reached #11 on the Hot 100 after she had left the Pointer Sisters to record for Motown herself, though her solo career never matched the subsequent commercial heights her former group achieved.

1979.title: Hold the Line
1979.fun_fact: Written by David Paich, who had played on Boz Scaggs's Silk Degrees sessions with several other future Toto members, this debut single reached #5 on the Hot 100 and introduced one of rock's most technically accomplished studio groups to mainstream audiences.

1979.title: He's the Greatest Dancer
1979.fun_fact: Written and produced by Nile Rodgers and Bernard Edwards of Chic, this reached #9 on the Hot 100 and was the first of two major hits Sister Sledge recorded with the Chic Organization, the second being 'We Are Family.'

1979.title: Sharing the Night Together
1979.fun_fact: Written by Ava Aldridge and Eddie Struzick, Dr. Hook's late-night romantic track reached #6 on the Hot 100 and was part of their most commercially successful period as they transitioned from novelty acts to sincere mainstream pop performers.

1979.title: She Believes in Me
1979.fun_fact: Written by Steve Gibb, son of the Bee Gees' Barry Gibb's cousin Maurice, Kenny Rogers's romantic ballad reached #5 on the Hot 100 and was part of his extraordinary commercial run that made him one of the best-selling recording artists of the late 1970s.

1979.title: In the Navy
1979.fun_fact: The United States Navy briefly considered using this as a recruitment tool and gave Village People access to naval vessels for filming before discovering the song's subtext; it reached #3 on the Hot 100 and the Navy ultimately withdrew their association.

1979.title: Music Box Dancer
1979.fun_fact: Canadian pianist Frank Mills composed this delicate instrumental in the early 1970s but it found its commercial moment when re-released in 1979, reaching #3 on the Hot 100 and becoming one of the best-selling instrumental singles of the decade.

1979.title: The Devil Went Down to Georgia
1979.fun_fact: Written in about an hour after Charlie Daniels started playing a fiddle riff, this spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100 and won the Grammy for Best Country Vocal Performance, becoming one of the most enduring story songs in American music history.

1979.title: Gold
1979.fun_fact: Former Kingston Trio member John Stewart co-wrote this with Nick Reynolds and it featured Stevie Nicks and Lindsey Buckingham on vocals; the track reached #5 on the Hot 100 in one of the more unexpected late-career commercial breakthroughs of the era.

1979.title: Goodnight Tonight
1979.fun_fact: Paul McCartney deliberately crafted this as a disco track to test whether he could write convincingly in the genre; it reached #5 on the Hot 100 and was the final major Wings hit before the group's effective dissolution.

1979.title: We Are Family
1979.fun_fact: Written and produced by Nile Rodgers and Bernard Edwards as an anthem of collective strength, this reached #2 on the Hot 100 and was adopted by the 1979 Pittsburgh Pirates as their championship theme, becoming one of sport's most enduring celebration songs.

1979.title: Rock 'n' Roll Fantasy
1979.fun_fact: Paul Rodgers wrote this meditation on the relationship between musicians and their fans, reaching #5 on the Hot 100 and representing one of Bad Company's more thoughtful and self-aware moments amid their usual hard-driving rock output.

1979.title: Every 1's a Winner
1979.fun_fact: Errol Brown wrote this optimistic disco track that reached #6 on the Hot 100 and was one of several Hot Chocolate recordings that crossed successfully to American audiences despite the group being primarily known for their extraordinary run of UK hits.

1979.title: Take Me Home
1979.fun_fact: Cher's disco reinvention reached #8 on the Hot 100 and was accompanied by a striking visual rebrand that foreshadowed the even more dramatic personal and professional transformations she would undertake in the 1980s.

1979.title: Boogie Wonderland
1979.fun_fact: Written by Jon Lind and Allee Willis, the collaboration between Earth Wind and Fire and the Emotions reached #6 on the Hot 100 and represented one of the final major Earth Wind and Fire recordings of their classic golden period.

1979.title: (Our Love) Don't Throw It All Away
1979.fun_fact: Written by Barry Gibb and Blue Weaver, this was Andy Gibb's final major US hit before personal struggles with substance abuse began to derail a career that had started with such extraordinary promise; it reached #9 on the Hot 100.

1979.title: What You Won't Do for Love
1979.fun_fact: Bobby Caldwell's label initially feared that revealing he was white would hurt the song's chances on R&B radio, so his image was deliberately withheld; the strategy worked, and the track reached #9 on the Hot 100 and #6 on the R&B chart.

1979.title: New York Groove
1979.fun_fact: KISS guitarist Ace Frehley's cover of Hello's 1975 glam rock track reached #13 on the Hot 100 and remains the most commercially successful solo recording by any member of KISS outside of their work together as a group.

1979.title: Sultans of Swing
1979.fun_fact: Mark Knopfler wrote this about watching an uninspiring pub band perform and the quiet dignity with which they played; it reached #4 on the Hot 100 and introduced Knopfler's distinctive fingerpicking guitar style to American audiences.

1979.title: I Want Your Love
1979.fun_fact: Nile Rodgers and Bernard Edwards of Chic continued their extraordinary creative run with this sophisticated disco track that reached #7 on the Hot 100, further demonstrating their ability to craft multiple compelling recordings within the same musical framework.

1979.title: Chuck E.'s in Love
1979.fun_fact: Rickie Lee Jones wrote this character study about a mutual friend's new romantic obsession; it reached #4 on the Hot 100 and introduced her jazz-influenced bohemian pop sensibility to mainstream audiences in one of the year's most critically acclaimed debut recordings.

1979.title: I Love the Nightlife
1979.fun_fact: Alicia Bridges's disco anthem charted in both 1978 and 1979, with its sustained popularity in clubs keeping it commercially active long after its initial chart run and demonstrating the gap between radio chart success and actual dance floor cultural impact.

1979.title: Ain't No Stoppin' Us Now
1979.fun_fact: Written by Gene McFadden, John Whitehead, and Jerry Cohen, this Philadelphia soul anthem reached #13 on the Hot 100 and has since become one of the most frequently played songs at sporting events, political rallies, and graduation ceremonies.

1979.title: Lonesome Loser
1979.fun_fact: Little River Band's second entry in the 1979 year-end Hot 100 reached #6 on the Hot 100 and confirmed their status as one of the most consistently successful Australian exports in American pop music history up to that point.

1979.title: Renegade
1979.fun_fact: Tommy Shaw wrote this as a first-person narrative from a condemned outlaw facing execution, and it reached #16 on the Hot 100 while becoming one of Styx's most beloved concert pieces, later used memorably as a television theme in It's Always Sunny in Philadelphia.

1979.title: Love Is the Answer
1979.fun_fact: Written by Todd Rundgren, England Dan and John Ford Coley's version reached #10 on the Hot 100 and was a marked stylistic departure from Rundgren's own recording, transforming a mid-tempo rock song into a smooth adult contemporary ballad.

1979.title: Got to Be Real
1979.fun_fact: Cheryl Lynn's debut single was produced by David Paich and David Foster and reached #12 on the Hot 100, introducing a powerhouse vocal talent who had auditioned unsuccessfully for Star Search before finding success through more traditional industry channels.

1979.title: Born to Be Alive
1979.fun_fact: French singer Patrick Hernandez's only hit spent twelve weeks at #1 in France before reaching #16 on the Hot 100; the song's recording sessions reportedly introduced Patrick Hernandez to a young woman named Madonna Ciccone who was working as a dancer.

1979.title: Shine a Little Love
1979.fun_fact: Jeff Lynne wrote this as ELO's most direct foray into pure disco territory, and it reached #8 on the Hot 100 from the Discovery album, which represented their most commercially successful but also most critically divisive exploration of contemporary dance music.

1979.title: I Just Fall in Love Again
1979.fun_fact: Written by Larry Herbstritt, Steve Dorff, Harry Lloyd, and Gloria Sklerov, Anne Murray's gentle country-pop ballad reached #12 on the Hot 100 and was part of her sustained adult contemporary success that made her one of the decade's most reliable chart performers.

1979.title: Shake It
1979.fun_fact: Former Fairport Convention member Ian Matthews scored his biggest solo hit with this track and reached #13 on the Hot 100; the song was a considerable commercial departure from the British folk rock that had defined his earlier career.

1979.title: I Was Made for Lovin' You
1979.fun_fact: Kiss's deliberate attempt to write a disco single reached #11 on the Hot 100 and was commercially successful but alienated many of their hard rock fans, who felt it represented a betrayal of the group's identity as a no-compromise heavy rock act.

1979.title: I Just Wanna Stop
1979.fun_fact: Canadian singer-songwriter Gino Vannelli wrote this dramatic ballad with his brother Joe and it reached #4 on the Hot 100, representing his biggest commercial moment in a career that had consistently prioritized artistic ambition over mainstream accessibility.

1979.title: Disco Nights (Rock-Freak)
1979.fun_fact: New York trio GQ created this funk-disco hybrid that reached #12 on the Hot 100 and was one of the final major disco hits to reach the top 20 before the format collapse that followed the infamous Disco Demolition Night at Comiskey Park in July 1979.

1979.title: Ooo Baby Baby
1979.fun_fact: Linda Ronstadt's tender cover of Smokey Robinson and the Miracles' 1965 Motown classic reached #7 on the Hot 100 and demonstrated her continuing ability to find new emotional depths in familiar pop material.

1979.title: September
1979.fun_fact: Written by Maurice White, Al McKay, and Allee Willis about the feeling of joy on a specific date, September 21st; Willis has said she doesn't know why White chose that date and he never fully explained it, giving the song a pleasant mystery that has sustained its popularity.

1979.title: Time Passages
1979.fun_fact: Al Stewart and Peter White wrote this meditation on memory and mortality for the album of the same name, and it reached #7 on the Hot 100 in a commercial follow-up that demonstrated 'Year of the Cat' had not been an isolated fluke.

1979.title: Rise
1979.fun_fact: Herb Alpert's jazz instrumental became one of the most unexpected #1 hits of 1979, spending two weeks at the top of the Hot 100 and later becoming one of the most sampled recordings in hip-hop history after Biggie Smalls used it for 'Hypnotize.'

1979.title: Don't Bring Me Down
1979.fun_fact: Jeff Lynne wrote this intentionally stripped-back rock track as a reaction to the ornate productions of their previous albums; it reached #4 on the Hot 100 and was one of the most successful experiments in self-imposed musical simplification in pop history.

1979.title: Promises
1979.fun_fact: Written by Richard Feldman and Roger Linn, Eric Clapton's polished adult contemporary recording reached #9 on the Hot 100 and represented his most commercially successful recording in years, achieved by embracing a softer sound quite different from his blues rock roots.

1979.title: Get Used to It
1979.fun_fact: Roger Voudouris's only major hit reached #21 on the Hot 100 with a smooth California sound influenced by the yacht rock genre that was becoming one of adult contemporary radio's dominant aesthetics in the late 1970s.

1979.title: How Much I Feel
1979.fun_fact: Written by David Pack about the emotional difficulty of a relationship he was ending, Ambrosia's sophisticated pop recording reached #3 on the Hot 100 and showcased the California band's ability to blend complex musical arrangements with emotionally direct songwriting.

1979.title: Suspicions
1979.fun_fact: Written by Eddie Rabbitt and Even Stevens, this country-pop track about romantic jealousy reached #13 on the Hot 100 and was part of Rabbitt's sustained commercial run that made him one of the most successful country crossover artists of the late 1970s.

1979.title: You Take My Breath Away
1979.fun_fact: Actor and singer Rex Smith recorded this for the television film Sooner or Later and reached #10 on the Hot 100 with a teen-idol appeal that briefly suggested he might sustain a career as both a performing artist and dramatic actor.

1979.title: How You Gonna See Me Now
1979.fun_fact: Alice Cooper wrote this about the difficulty of reconnecting with family after his very public struggle with alcoholism; it reached #12 on the Hot 100 and was one of his most personally vulnerable recordings, marking a period of genuine emotional reckoning.

1979.title: Double Vision
1979.fun_fact: Mick Jones and Lou Gramm wrote this while Gramm was suffering from actual double vision due to exhaustion during a tour; it reached #2 on the Hot 100 and became one of Foreigner's most recognizable arena rock recordings.

1979.title: Everytime I Think of You
1979.fun_fact: British rock group The Babys, featuring a young John Waite on lead vocals, reached #13 on the Hot 100 with this melodic hard rock ballad that foreshadowed the AOR arena rock sound that would dominate the early 1980s.

1979.title: I Got My Mind Made Up (You Can Get It Girl)
1979.fun_fact: This Philadelphia funk group's debut single reached #20 on the Hot 100 and was one of the final recordings from the Philadelphia International Records orbit to achieve mainstream pop success before the disco backlash reshaped radio formats.

1979.title: Don't Stop 'til You Get Enough
1979.fun_fact: Michael Jackson's first solo single following his work with the Jacksons spent one week at #1 on the Hot 100 and announced the beginning of his solo adult career with an irresistibly complex falsetto-driven disco-funk performance that hinted at the genius of Thriller.

1979.title: Bad Case of Loving You (Doctor, Doctor)
1979.fun_fact: Written by Moon Martin, Robert Palmer's hard-driving rock track reached #14 on the Hot 100 and was the commercial breakthrough that established him as a viable album rock radio artist after years of critically praised but commercially modest recordings.

1979.title: Somewhere in the Night
1979.fun_fact: Written by Richard Kerr and Will Jennings, Barry Manilow's recording of this late-night longing ballad reached #9 on the Hot 100 and continued his run as adult contemporary radio's most reliable hitmaker throughout the decade's final years.

1979.title: We've Got Tonight
1979.artist: Bob Seger & The Silver Bullet Band
1979.fun_fact: Bob Seger wrote this in the aftermath of a relationship's end, capturing the bittersweet feeling of one final night together; it reached #13 on the Hot 100 and was later covered by Kenny Rogers and Sheena Easton for an even bigger commercial hit.

1979.title: Dance the Night Away
1979.fun_fact: Eddie Van Halen's brother Alex created the drum pattern as a joke, but the band kept it and the track became their first top 15 pop hit, reaching #15 on the Hot 100 and demonstrating that Van Halen's appeal extended beyond their core hard rock audience.

1979.title: Dancin' Shoes
1979.fun_fact: Elton John's longtime drummer Nigel Olsson scored his only significant solo hit with this Elton-influenced pop track that reached #18 on the Hot 100, capitalizing on the extended family of musicians who had developed their skills working within the Elton John organization.

1979.title: The Boss
1979.fun_fact: Written by Nickolas Ashford and Valerie Simpson, Diana Ross's disco-era declaration of independence reached #19 on the Hot 100 and captured her at her most assertively contemporary, embracing the dance floor sound that was dominating the charts during her commercial comeback.

1979.title: Sail On
1979.fun_fact: Written by Lionel Richie as a country-influenced ballad about accepting the end of a relationship with grace, this reached #4 on the Hot 100 and was part of the Commodores' deliberate strategy of balancing their funk identity with the ballads Richie was beginning to specialize in.

1979.title: I Do Love You
1979.fun_fact: GQ's second entry in the 1979 year-end Hot 100 reached #42 on the Hot 100 but charted strongly enough in R&B and dance formats to make the year-end list, confirming the group's brief but notable commercial moment in the disco era's final months.

1979.title: Strange Way
1979.fun_fact: Written by Rick Roberts, the Colorado soft rock group Firefall reached #11 on the Hot 100 with this melodic country-rock ballad, one of several recordings that demonstrated the continuing commercial viability of the California country-rock sound into the late disco era.
